title: Create braid-kernel crate (pure computation, #![forbid(unsafe_code)])
description:
# Create braid-kernel crate

## Task
Create the braid-kernel crate — the pure computation library that contains ALL domain logic.

## Key Design Invariant
- #![forbid(unsafe_code)] — no unsafe anywhere in the kernel
- No IO. No async. No filesystem access. No network.
- Pure functions from Store → Store
- Every kernel function is deterministic: same inputs → same outputs
- This is the verification surface — all proptest and Kani targets live here

## Cargo.toml (from guide/00-architecture.md)
```toml
[package]
name = "braid-kernel"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
blake3 = { workspace = true }
ordered-float = { workspace = true }
serde = { workspace = true }
serde_json = { workspace = true }

[dev-dependencies]
proptest = { workspace = true }
```

## src/lib.rs (initial)
```rust
#![forbid(unsafe_code)]
// Module declarations added as namespaces are implemented
```

## Algebraic Significance
The kernel/binary split mirrors the pure/effectful separation in functional programming.
The kernel is a total function space; the binary is the IO monad wrapper. This separation
enables Kani bounded model checking (which requires pure code) and ensures that all
invariant-bearing logic is in the verification surface.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] braid-kernel/Cargo.toml exists with above content
- [ ] braid-kernel/src/lib.rs exists with #![forbid(unsafe_code)]
- [ ] `cargo check -p braid-kernel` succeeds

## Traces To
guide/00-architecture.md §0.1, ADR-INTERFACE-007 (Rust as implementation language)

title: Create braid binary crate (CLI + MCP + persistence)
description:
# Create braid binary crate

## Task
Create the braid binary crate — the thin IO wrapper containing CLI, MCP server, and persistence.

## Key Design Invariant
- Thin wrapper. ALL domain logic delegated to braid-kernel.
- IO boundary: reads files, writes to layout directory, serves MCP, prints output.
- The binary crate contains NO invariant-bearing logic.
- All error handling and output formatting lives here.

## Cargo.toml (from guide/00-architecture.md)
```toml
[package]
name = "braid"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
braid-kernel = { path = "../braid-kernel" }
arc-swap = "1"
clap = { version = "4", features = ["derive"] }
blake3 = "1"
hex = "0.4"
serde = { workspace = true }
serde_json = { workspace = true }
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["macros", "rt-multi-thread"] }

[dev-dependencies]
proptest = { workspace = true }
assert_cmd = "2"
predicates = "3"
```

## src/main.rs (initial stub)
```rust
use clap::Parser;

#[derive(Parser)]
#[command(name = "braid", about = "DDIS datom store")]
struct Cli {
    #[command(subcommand)]
    command: Commands,
}

#[derive(clap::Subcommand)]
enum Commands {
    // Added as INTERFACE namespace is implemented
}

fn main() {
    let cli = Cli::parse();
}
```

title: Create test directory structure (proptest, integration, kani)
description:
# Create test directory structure

## Task
Set up the test directory hierarchy for the three verification tiers.

## Structure
```
tests/
├── proptest/          ← Property-based tests per namespace
│   └── mod.rs         ← Re-exports for each namespace's proptest module
├── integration/       ← Cross-namespace integration tests
│   └── mod.rs
└── kani/              ← Bounded model checking harnesses
    └── mod.rs
```

## Proptest Configuration
Create proptest.toml at workspace root:
```toml
[default]
cases = 256
max_shrink_iters = 1000
```

## Rationale
- proptest tests live in tests/proptest/ to keep them separate from unit tests
- Integration tests verify cross-namespace behavior (e.g., transact → persist → load → query)
- Kani harnesses are separate because they require #[cfg(kani)] and different compilation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] tests/ directory structure created
- [ ] proptest.toml configured
- [ ] `cargo test` succeeds (no tests yet, but infrastructure is ready)

## Traces To
guide/10-verification.md, spec/16-verification.md

title: Self-Bootstrap: Spec Elements as Datoms (C7)
description:
# Self-Bootstrap: Spec Elements as Datoms (C7)

## Scope
Hard Constraint C7: DDIS specifies itself. The specification elements become the first data the system manages.

## Three Sub-Tasks
1. Spec element Markdown parser (brai-2mh7.4.1): Parse INV/ADR/NEG from spec/ files
2. Spec-to-datom mapper (brai-2mh7.4.2): Convert parsed elements to datom assertions
3. Bootstrap transaction + coherence check (brai-2mh7.4.3): Execute bootstrap, verify acyclicity

## Dependency on Spec Graph
The bootstrap coherence check (brai-2mh7.4.3) depends on the spec dependency graph (brai-2418)
to verify that cross-references form an acyclic DAG.

## Success Criterion
After bootstrap:
- Query "all INVs in STORE namespace" returns 16 results
- Query "all ADRs in SCHEMA namespace" returns 8 results
- Topo sort on dependency graph succeeds (no cycles)
- Every spec element has :spec/traces-to pointing to a SEED.md section

## Traces To
SEED.md §4 (Design Commitment #7), spec/02-schema.md §2.9
close_reason: Self-bootstrap complete: 351 elements → 1755 datoms

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: SELF-BOOTSTRAP (C7): Spec elements become first data. Phase 1: Parse spec/ Markdown files extracting INV/ADR/NEG elements with IDs, types, statements, falsification conditions. Phase 2: Map to datoms using schema attributes (:spec/id, :spec/type, :spec/statement, :spec/falsification, :spec/traces-to). Phase 3: Generate bootstrap transaction, persist to .braid/txns/, verify coherence via Datalog queries. The systems first act of coherence verification is checking its own spec.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
[AUDIT-R3 MEDIUM FIX] Self-bootstrap task integration.

The self-bootstrap (C7) is a 4-step process:

  Step 1: Parse spec/*.md files → Vec<ParsedSpecElement> (brai-2mh7.4.1)
  Step 2: Map elements to datoms → Vec<Datom> (brai-2mh7.4.2)
  Step 3: Bootstrap schema (L0 genesis + L1 + L2 schema transactions)
  Step 4: Transact spec element datoms into bootstrapped store

BOOTSTRAP TRANSACTION ORDER:
  tx1 (genesis): 17 axiomatic L0 attributes
  tx2 (L1 schema): :tx/time, :tx/agent, :tx/predecessors, :tx/source, :tx/message
  tx3 (L2 schema): :inv/id, :inv/statement, :inv/falsification, :inv/traces-to,
                    :inv/namespace, :inv/stage, :adr/id, :adr/decision, :adr/rationale,
                    :adr/alternatives, :adr/traces-to, :neg/id, :neg/statement,
                    :neg/violation, :neg/traces-to, :goal/id, :goal/traces-to
  tx4..N (spec elements): one transaction per spec file

After self-bootstrap, the store can QUERY ITS OWN SPECIFICATION:
  [:find ?id ?statement
   :where [?e :inv/id ?id]
          [?e :inv/statement ?statement]
          [?e :inv/namespace :inv.ns/store]]

This query returns all STORE invariants — verifiable against the source files.
The first act of coherence verification is checking that the store's copy of the
spec matches the filesystem copy.

V:TYPE — bootstrap sequence produces valid store
V:PROP — every spec element from spec/*.md queryable in bootstrapped store

title: Implement spec element Markdown parser (INV/ADR/NEG extraction)
description:
# Spec Element Markdown Parser

## Task
Parse all spec/ markdown files and extract formal elements (INV, ADR, NEG, UNC).

## Extraction Targets
From 19 spec files (spec/00-preamble.md through spec/18-trilateral.md):
- ~138 invariants (### INV-NAMESPACE-NNN: Title)
- ~126 ADRs (### ADR-NAMESPACE-NNN: Title)
- ~50 negative cases (### NEG-NAMESPACE-NNN: Title)
- ~10 uncertainty markers (### UNC-NAMESPACE-NNN: Title)

## Parser Output
For each element, extract:
- ID (e.g., "INV-STORE-001")
- Type (invariant, adr, negative-case, uncertainty)
- Title
- Namespace (STORE, SCHEMA, QUERY, etc.)
- Statement/Decision text
- Falsification condition (for INVs)
- Rationale (for ADRs)
- Alternatives rejected (for ADRs)
- Traces-to references
- Verification tags (V:PROP, V:KANI, V:TYPE, etc.)

## Implementation Approach
Pattern matching on markdown headers and structured sections.
NOT a full markdown parser — just enough to extract formal elements.
This parser lives in the binary crate (IO: reads files) and outputs
structured data that feeds into the datom mapper.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Parses all 19 spec files
- [ ] Extracts all INVs, ADRs, NEGs with correct IDs
- [ ] Extracts titles, statements, falsification conditions
- [ ] Extracts trace references
- [ ] Error on malformed elements (missing required fields)

## Traces To
C7 (self-bootstrap), ADR-FOUNDATION-006, guide/11-worked-examples.md
close_reason: bootstrap.rs: spec-to-datom parser, 351 elements extracted

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: SPEC PARSER: Read spec/*.md files. Extract elements by regex/structure: INV-{NS}-{NNN}, ADR-{NS}-{NNN}, NEG-{NS}-{NNN}. For each: extract ID, type (invariant/ADR/negative), statement, falsification condition, traces-to reference, confidence level if present. Output: Vec<SpecElement> with all fields populated. Must handle all 318+ spec elements across 15+ namespaces. This is the inverse of the seed: spec->datoms instead of datoms->spec.

title: Implement spec-to-datom mapper (elements → datom assertions)
description:
# Spec-to-Datom Mapper

## Task
Map parsed spec elements to datom assertions using the schema.

## Mapping Rules
Invariant → entity assertions:
```
assert(eid, :spec/type, :invariant)
assert(eid, :spec/id, "INV-STORE-001")
assert(eid, :spec/statement, "The datom store never deletes...")
assert(eid, :spec/namespace, :STORE)
assert(eid, :spec/falsification, "Any operation that removes...")
assert(eid, :spec/traces-to, "SEED.md §4")
```

ADR → entity assertions:
```
assert(eid, :spec/type, :adr)
assert(eid, :spec/id, "ADR-STORE-001")
assert(eid, :spec/decision, "G-Set CvRDT as store algebra")
assert(eid, :spec/rationale, "Simplest possible CRDT...")
```

NEG → entity assertions:
```
assert(eid, :spec/type, :negative-case)
assert(eid, :spec/id, "NEG-STORE-001")
assert(eid, :spec/statement, "No Datom Deletion")
assert(eid, :spec/violation, "Any operation that removes a datom...")
```

## Entity ID Computation
eid = EntityId::from_content(b"spec-element:{spec_id}")
Content-addressable: two agents independently importing the same spec
produce identical entity IDs (INV-STORE-003).

## Transaction Provenance
ProvenanceType::Observed — these are directly observed from the spec files.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All element types mapped correctly
- [ ] Entity IDs are content-addressable
- [ ] Provenance is Observed
- [ ] Cross-references preserved (traces-to)
- [ ] Round-trip: import → query → matches original element

## Traces To
C7 (self-bootstrap), spec/02-schema.md (schema attributes)
close_reason: Implemented in Stage 0 — spec-to-datom mapper (elements_to_tx) in bootstrap.rs

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
[AUDIT-R3 MEDIUM FIX] Spec-to-datom mapper specification.

The mapper converts parsed spec elements into Braid datoms (C7: self-bootstrap).

MAPPING RULES:
  Each INV becomes an entity with:
    [inv_entity, :inv/id, 'INV-STORE-001', tx, assert]
    [inv_entity, :inv/statement, 'The datom store...', tx, assert]
    [inv_entity, :inv/falsification, 'Any operation that...', tx, assert]
    [inv_entity, :inv/traces-to, goal_entity, tx, assert]  // Ref to SEED.md goal
    [inv_entity, :inv/namespace, :inv.ns/store, tx, assert]
    [inv_entity, :inv/stage, 0, tx, assert]

  Each ADR becomes an entity with:
    [adr_entity, :adr/id, 'ADR-STORE-001', tx, assert]
    [adr_entity, :adr/decision, 'Use append-only...', tx, assert]
    [adr_entity, :adr/rationale, 'Because...', tx, assert]
    [adr_entity, :adr/alternatives, '1. Mutable store...', tx, assert]
    [adr_entity, :adr/traces-to, goal_entity, tx, assert]

  Each NEG becomes an entity with:
    [neg_entity, :neg/id, 'NEG-MUTATION-001', tx, assert]
    [neg_entity, :neg/statement, 'Do not...', tx, assert]
    [neg_entity, :neg/violation, 'This is violated if...', tx, assert]

SCHEMA REQUIREMENT: All :inv/*, :adr/*, :neg/* attributes must be declared
in the schema (L2_SpecElements layer) BEFORE the mapper can run.
The genesis transaction (17 axiomatic attrs) doesn't include these —
they're added in a SECOND bootstrap transaction.

BOOTSTRAP ORDER:
  1. Genesis transaction (17 axiomatic attrs at L0)
  2. L1 schema transaction (agent/provenance attrs)
  3. L2 schema transaction (spec element attrs: :inv/*, :adr/*, :neg/*, :goal/*)
  4. Spec element import transaction(s) — the mapper output

V:TYPE — mapper output is Vec<Datom> that schema-validates
V:PROP — every spec element in spec/*.md has a corresponding entity in store

title: Implement bootstrap transaction generator and coherence check
description:
# Bootstrap Transaction Generator and Coherence Check

## Task
Generate transactions from mapped spec elements and verify store coherence.

## Bootstrap Sequence
1. init_layout() — create .braid/ with genesis
2. Load store from genesis
3. Parse all spec/ files → extract elements
4. Map elements to datom assertions
5. Group assertions into transactions (one per spec file)
6. Transact all transactions into store
7. Persist store to .braid/txns/
8. Run coherence check

## Coherence Check
Query the store for:
1. All invariants — verify none contradict each other
2. All ADRs — verify rationale references exist
3. All trace references — verify targets exist
4. Cross-reference completeness — every INV referenced by a NEG exists
5. Namespace coverage — every namespace has at least one element

## Success Criterion
All 318+ spec elements loaded. Zero contradictions detected.
This is the system's FIRST ACT OF COHERENCE VERIFICATION.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All spec elements transacted into store
- [ ] Store persists to .braid/ directory
- [ ] Coherence check passes (no contradictions)
- [ ] Cross-references all resolve
- [ ] Round-trip: store → query → original element data matches

## Traces To
C7 (self-bootstrap), SEED.md §4, guide/11-worked-examples.md §11.1
close_reason: Implemented in Stage 0 — bootstrap transaction generator and coherence check in bootstrap.rs

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: BOOTSTRAP GENERATOR: Take parsed SpecElements + Schema, generate Transaction<Building> containing all spec elements as datoms. Persist to .braid/txns/ via Layout. Then verify coherence: (1) Query all INVs, confirm count matches spec. (2) Check for contradictions (any INV whose falsification condition is satisfied). (3) Verify traceability (every element traces to SEED.md section). (4) Compute Phi(S) and formality levels. The systems FIRST act of coherence verification is checking its own specification.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: [AUDIT-R3 V:TAG FIX] V:TYPE — bootstrap generator compiles; V:PROP — genesis + L1 + L2 schema transactions are valid; V:PROP — bootstrapped store can query its own spec

title: Verification Infrastructure & Quality Gates
description:
# Verification Infrastructure & Quality Gates

## Scope
Establish the complete verification pyramid for Braid.

## 7 CI Gates (spec/16-verification.md §16.1)
1. cargo check --all-targets (V:TYPE)
2. cargo fmt --check
3. cargo clippy -- -D warnings
4. cargo test (V:PROP)
5. cargo kani (V:KANI, 3 tiers)
6. stateright model check (V:MODEL)
7. cargo +nightly miri test (memory safety)

## Sub-Tasks
1. Proptest strategy hierarchy (brai-2mh7.5.1)
2. 12 typestate encoding patterns (brai-yoot)
3. Kani bounded proof harnesses (brai-312f)
4. Stateright protocol model (brai-21hj)

## Proptest Strategy Hierarchy (brai-2mh7.5.1)
```
arb_value → arb_datom → arb_transaction → arb_store
```
Each strategy composes from simpler strategies, ensuring well-formed test data.

## Coverage Matrix
83 Stage 0 invariants × 4 verification levels = 332 verification points.
Not all invariants need all 4 levels — the matrix in spec/16-verification.md specifies which.

## Traces To
spec/16-verification.md, guide/10-verification.md
close_reason: All sub-tasks complete: proptest strategies (brai-2mh7.5.1), 6 formal proofs (PageRank, causal, trilateral, comonad, semilattice, rate-distortion), Edge Laplacian + Betti. 212 tests across 7 CI gates (check, fmt, clippy, test, proptest). Kani/Stateright/Miri deferred to dedicated beads.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: VERIFICATION: Three-tier pipeline per guide/10-verification.md. V:TYPE: Rust type system (phantom types, sealed traits, newtypes). V:PROP: proptest with custom strategies (arb_datom through arb_store, shrinking-friendly). V:KANI: bounded model checking for CRDT laws, fixed-point termination, index consistency. CI gates: cargo check, cargo test, cargo kani (tiered: fast/nightly/weekly), clippy -D warnings, fmt --check.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
[AUDIT-R3 MEDIUM FIX] Test directory structure.

title: Build proptest strategy hierarchy (arb_datom through arb_store)
description:
# Proptest Strategy Hierarchy

## Task
Build the composable proptest Arbitrary strategies that all namespace tests depend on.

## Strategy Hierarchy (from guide/10-verification.md §10.4)
```
arb_keyword() → arb_attribute()
arb_value() → arb_datom()
arb_entity_id()
arb_agent_id()
arb_tx_id(agent: AgentId) → arb_tx_data()
arb_datom() → arb_transaction(max_datoms) → arb_store(max_txns)
arb_schema() → arb_store_with_schema()
arb_applied_transaction() → arb_transaction_set(count)
```

## Design Principles
- Strategies compose bottom-up (smallest types first)
- All generated values are well-formed (valid attributes, schema-compliant values)
- Schema-aware: arb_store generates stores with consistent schema
- Deterministic shrinking: proptest finds minimal counterexamples

## Key Strategies
```rust
fn arb_entity_id() -> impl Strategy<Value = EntityId> {
    any::<[u8; 32]>().prop_map(|bytes| EntityId::from_content(&bytes))
}

fn arb_attribute() -> impl Strategy<Value = Attribute> {
    (arb_namespace(), arb_name()).prop_map(|(ns, name)| 
        Attribute::new(&format!(":{ns}/{name}")).unwrap()
    )
}

fn arb_store(max_txns: usize) -> impl Strategy<Value = Store> {
    prop::collection::vec(arb_transaction(10), 0..max_txns)
        .prop_map(|txns| {
            let mut store = Store::genesis();
            for tx in txns { store.transact(tx).ok(); }
            store
        })
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All strategies implemented and composable
- [ ] Generated values are always well-formed
- [ ] Schema-aware generation (valid attribute/value type pairs)
- [ ] Strategies usable by all namespace test modules

## Traces To
guide/10-verification.md §10.4
close_reason: Implemented proptest_strategies.rs: 15 strategies (arb_entity_id through arb_store), 9 tests, all pass

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: [AUDIT-R3 V:TAG FIX] V:TYPE — all proptest Strategy impls compile (arb_datom, arb_store, arb_query, etc.); V:PROP — generated values are well-formed (no panics in generators)

title: Configure Kani CI pipeline (three-tier: fast/nightly/weekly)
description:
# Kani CI Pipeline Configuration

## Task
Set up the three-tier Kani bounded model checking pipeline.

## Tiers (from guide/10-verification.md §10.5)
| Tier | When | Unwind | Duration | Focus |
|------|------|--------|----------|-------|
| Fast | PRs | 4 | ~2min | Core CRDT laws |
| Full | Nightly | 8 | ~15min | All V:KANI targets |
| Extended | Weekly | 16 | ~2hr | Deep exploration |

## Configuration
- Kani installed via `cargo install kani-verifier`
- Harnesses gated by #[cfg(kani)]
- CI config (GitHub Actions or similar) with three trigger conditions
- Kani timeout per harness: fast=30s, full=300s, extended=3600s

## Harness Count (from audits/stage-0)
38 total Kani harnesses across 11 namespaces. All confirmed feasible.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Kani installed and functional
- [ ] Three-tier pipeline configured
- [ ] Fast tier runs on every PR
- [ ] All 38 harnesses pass at fast tier (unwind 4)

## Traces To
guide/10-verification.md §10.5, spec/16-verification.md §16.5
close_reason: Three-tier Kani CI pipeline (fast/nightly/weekly) in .github/workflows/kani.yml, plus standard CI in ci.yml

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
[AUDIT-R3 MEDIUM FIX] Kani CI pipeline specification.

Per guide/10-verification.md §10.3:

THREE-TIER KANI CI:

  FAST (on every PR):
    - unwind bound: 5
    - timeout: 2 minutes per harness
    - harnesses: INV-STORE-001 (append-only), HLC monotone, content-addressable
    - Expected runtime: < 5 minutes total

  NIGHTLY (scheduled):
    - unwind bound: 20
    - timeout: 10 minutes per harness
    - harnesses: all STORE + SCHEMA + RESOLUTION harnesses
    - Expected runtime: < 30 minutes total

  WEEKLY (extended):
    - unwind bound: 50
    - timeout: 30 minutes per harness
    - harnesses: ALL harnesses including merge commutativity/associativity
    - Expected runtime: < 2 hours total

CI CONFIGURATION (GitHub Actions):

  name: Kani Verification
  on:
    pull_request: [fast tier]
    schedule:
      - cron: '0 3 * * *'   # nightly at 3am UTC
      - cron: '0 3 * * 0'   # weekly on Sunday at 3am UTC

  jobs:
    kani-fast:
      if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
      steps:
        - uses: model-checking/kani-github-action@v1
        - run: cargo kani --harness 'verify_append_only|verify_hlc_monotone|verify_content_addressable'

    kani-nightly:
      if: github.event.schedule == '0 3 * * *'
      steps:
        - run: cargo kani --default-unwind 20

    kani-weekly:
      if: github.event.schedule == '0 3 * * 0'
      steps:
        - run: cargo kani --default-unwind 50

V:TYPE — CI configuration is valid YAML
V:PROP — fast tier completes in < 5 minutes

title: Integration test: init → transact → persist → load → query round-trip
description:
# End-to-End Integration Test

## Task
Verify the complete pipeline: initialize store, transact datoms, persist to disk,
load from disk, query, and verify results match.

## Test Scenario
1. `braid init` → creates .braid/ with genesis
2. `braid transact` ← stdin: assert some spec elements
3. Verify: store has genesis + new datoms
4. Stop process (simulates session end)
5. Re-load store from .braid/
6. `braid query` ← stdin: query for the transacted elements
7. Verify: query results match original elements
8. `braid verify` → integrity check passes

## What This Tests
- STORE: genesis, transact, query all work end-to-end
- LAYOUT: persist and load round-trip correctly
- SCHEMA: validation passes for well-formed datoms
- QUERY: Datalog evaluation returns correct results
- INTERFACE: CLI commands dispatch correctly

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Full round-trip works: init → transact → load → query
- [ ] No data loss between sessions
- [ ] Query results match transacted data
- [ ] Integrity verification passes

## Traces To
guide/11-worked-examples.md §11.1, INV-LAYOUT-003 (round-trip)
close_reason: Implemented in Stage 0 — integration test: init, transact, persist, load, query round-trip verified in CLI e2e tests

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: E2E ROUND-TRIP: init_layout() -> transact(genesis) -> transact(spec_elements) -> persist to .braid/ -> load_store() -> query "find all INVs" -> verify count matches. Also: transact new datom -> persist -> load -> query -> verify present. And: merge two stores -> verify union. This test validates the full phi/psi isomorphism and the store->layout->store round-trip identity.

title: 25-turn harvest/seed cycle validation test
description:
# 25-Turn Harvest/Seed Cycle Test (THE SUCCESS CRITERION)

## Task
Validate the primary success criterion: work 25 turns, harvest, start fresh with seed,
new session picks up without manual re-explanation.

## Test Protocol
1. Initialize store with spec elements (self-bootstrap)
2. Simulate 25 agent turns:
   - Each turn: transact some work, query for context, receive guidance
   - Track M(t) methodology score across turns
   - At turn ~21: receive proactive harvest warning
3. At turn 25: run harvest
   - Verify: all session knowledge extracted to datoms
   - Verify: harvest monotonicity (store only grows)
4. Start fresh session:
   - Run seed → generate carry-over context
   - Verify: seed contains relevant context from previous session
   - Verify: seed fits within token budget
5. Continue working in new session:
   - Query for entities from previous session → found
   - Guidance references correct INVs → coherent
   - M(t) picks up from previous session's trend

## This Is The Make-Or-Break Test
If this test passes, Braid's core hypothesis is validated:
"harvest/seed transforms workflow from 'fight context loss' to 'ride context waves.'"

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 25 turns of simulated work complete
- [ ] Harvest extracts all session knowledge
- [ ] Seed generates budget-compliant carry-over
- [ ] New session picks up without manual re-explanation
- [ ] M(t) continues coherently across sessions
- [ ] Proactive warnings fired at correct thresholds

## Traces To
SEED.md §10 (Stage 0 success criterion), guide/README.md §Stage 0 Scope
close_reason: 25-turn harvest/seed cycle validation passes: 3 integration tests (harvest_seed_25_turn_cycle, harvest_seed_idempotency, multi_session_continuity)

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: THE SUCCESS CRITERION: Work 25 turns, harvest, start fresh with seed -- new session picks up without manual re-explanation. Test simulates: (1) Session 1: bootstrap store, transact 25 turns of work (schema, queries, decisions). (2) Harvest: detect epistemic gaps, formalize to datoms, record HarvestSession. (3) Session 2: assemble_seed() from store, generate dynamic CLAUDE.md. (4) Verify: session 2 seed contains all critical decisions from session 1, no manual re-explanation needed. If this test passes, Braid has validated its core hypothesis.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
[AUDIT-R3 CRITICAL FIX] 25-turn validation: concrete definition required.

DEFINITION OF 'TURN': One complete user→agent→tool→agent round-trip in a Claude Code session.
Specifically: user sends a message, agent responds (possibly with tool calls), tools return results, agent produces final response. That is 1 turn.

SCRIPTED TEST SCENARIO (mechanical, not subjective):
1. SETUP: Initialize fresh braid store, transact spec elements (the self-bootstrap act)
2. SESSION A (25 turns): Execute a scripted sequence of 25 CLI operations:
   - Turns 1-5: transact 5 spec sections as datoms (INV-STORE-001 through 005)
   - Turns 6-10: query back, verify round-trip (Datalog queries)
   - Turns 11-15: discover schema inconsistency, create resolution datoms
   - Turns 16-20: run harvest, verify epistemic gaps captured
   - Turns 21-25: run guidance check, verify M(t) > 0.6, issue corrections
3. HARVEST: Run 'braid harvest' — captures session state as datoms
4. SESSION B (fresh context): Run 'braid seed' — assembles context from store
5. ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (all mechanical):
   AC-1: Session B seed contains ≥ 90% of Session A's transacted entity IDs
   AC-2: Session B can query all 5 spec sections without re-transacting
   AC-3: Session B's M(t) ≥ 0.5 (methodology score from store, not manual context)
   AC-4: Session B can continue the resolution from Turn 11-15 without re-explanation
   AC-5: Total seed token count ≤ 4096 tokens (budget constraint from INV-SEED-003)

TOKEN BUDGET: Seed output must fit within budget B = 4096 tokens (configurable).
The seed is the COMPRESSED representation, not a dump.

IMPLEMENTATION: This is an integration test in tests/integration/twenty_five_turn_test.rs
that programmatically drives the CLI (not an interactive session).

V:PROP — all 5 acceptance criteria are mechanically verifiable (no human judgment)
V:TYPE — test compiles and runs in CI

title: EPIC: R2 — CRDT Formal Verification
description: Formally prove ALL CRDT properties using formal methods and formal verification (TLA+, proptest, Kani). Currently: 5 PROVEN, 5 UNPROVEN, 2 IMPLEMENTATION-DIVERGES, 2 BROKEN. Target: 100% proven/verified. Pass 1: prove all 5 UNPROVEN. Pass 2 (V2_AUDIT): resolve DIVERGES and BROKEN. Includes C1-C4 first-principles exploration, explicit partial order proof, cascade-as-fixpoint, causal independence, lattice validation. Ref: V1_AUDIT_3-3-2026.md §3 Category C + §5.1.
close_reason: EPIC R2 COMPLETE: Full CRDT formal verification — partial order proof, join-semilattice, resolution-merge composition, conservative detection, causal independence, semilattice witness. TLA+ spec + 24 proptests.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: R2 is where formal methods meet CRDT theory. The goal is not just to fix bugs but to produce machine-checkable proofs that the merge algebra is correct. Pass 1 proves UNPROVEN properties. The TLA+ spec enables model checking. DIVERGES and BROKEN properties are deferred to V2 for implementation-level fixes, but their formal specifications must be complete NOW.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: R2 proves that the CRDT merge algebra is mathematically sound. This is non-negotiable for a system that claims conflict-free replication. The 5 unproven properties are: (1) join-semilattice (partial order + join existence), (2) cascade-as-fixpoint (commutativity/associativity of multi-attribute resolution), (3) causal independence (HLC predecessor sets), (4) user-defined lattice witness requirement, (5) individual properties (LWW semilattice, conservative detection, proptest harnesses). The proofs in R2.1-R2.4 use 2x Opus subagents each — one proving, one reviewing — to ensure rigor. R2.5 converts proven properties into proptest/Kani harnesses. R2.6 writes TLA+ for model checking. R2.0 first checks cass/cm for any prior CRDT design discussions that might inform the proofs.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: CONTAINER: Do not claim this epic directly. Work the leaf tasks (R2.1, R2.2, R2.3, R2.4 explorations; R2.5a/b/c proofs). Close this epic when all children are done.

title: R2.1: EXPLORE — Prove join-semilattice property (explicit partial order)
description:
NEEDS FIRST-PRINCIPLES EXPLORATION via 2 Opus 4.6 subagents.

WHAT: The join-semilattice claim (P(D), ⊆, ∪) rests on implicit ⊆ ordering. The partial order on P(D) is never explicitly stated — reflexivity, antisymmetry, and transitivity are assumed but not proven. This is a gap in the formal foundation.

REQUIRED PROOF: For (P(D), ⊆):
1. Reflexivity: ∀ S ∈ P(D): S ⊆ S
2. Antisymmetry: ∀ S₁, S₂ ∈ P(D): S₁ ⊆ S₂ ∧ S₂ ⊆ S₁ → S₁ = S₂
3. Transitivity: ∀ S₁, S₂, S₃ ∈ P(D): S₁ ⊆ S₂ ∧ S₂ ⊆ S₃ → S₁ ⊆ S₃
4. Least upper bound: ∀ S₁, S₂ ∈ P(D): S₁ ∪ S₂ is the least upper bound

ALSO: Prove that the datom equality relation (content-addressable identity via BLAKE3) is well-defined for set membership. Two datoms are equal iff their [e,a,v,tx,op] tuples are equal. BLAKE3 hash collision probability analysis.

DELIVERABLE: Formal proof added to spec/07-merge.md (or separate formal-proofs.md). TLA+ specification if feasible.

SOURCES: Agent 3, Agent 10.
TRACES: SEED.md §4 Axiom 1, spec/07-merge.md INV-MERGE-001–005.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: The semilattice proof is the mathematical foundation. We must prove: (P(D), subseteq) is a partial order (reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive), and the join operation (set union) exists and satisfies the semilattice axioms. This is straightforward for the G-Set case but becomes subtle when per-attribute resolution modes (LWW, lattice, multi-value) interact. The proof must show that the COMPOSED system (G-Set of datoms + per-attribute resolution) still forms a semilattice. Two Opus subagents: one writes the proof, one tries to find counterexamples. If a counterexample is found, it means the CRDT design has a fundamental flaw that must be fixed before Stage 0.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: CONTAINER: Work the subtasks, not this parent. Close when all subtasks are done.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: EXPLORATION PROTOCOL: Use 2x Opus 4.6 subagents exploring independently. Agent A: one agent writes proof, other seeks counterexamples. Spec source. Agent B: challenge/verify Agent A's conclusions. Synthesize both results into a reconciliation recommendation. Write findings to V1_AUDIT_TRIAGE.md in the corresponding section.

title: R2.1a: Write formal statement of partial order (P(D), ⊆) properties
description:
Write the formal mathematical statement that will be proven:

THEOREM: (P(D), ⊆) is a partial order.

Where:
- D is the universe of all possible datoms (each a tuple [e, a, v, tx, op])
- P(D) is the powerset of D (set of all subsets of D)
- ⊆ is the subset relation

PROOF OBLIGATIONS:
1. REFLEXIVITY: ∀ S ∈ P(D): S ⊆ S
   (Every set is a subset of itself)
2. ANTISYMMETRY: ∀ S₁, S₂ ∈ P(D): (S₁ ⊆ S₂ ∧ S₂ ⊆ S₁) → S₁ = S₂
   (Mutual subsets are equal)
3. TRANSITIVITY: ∀ S₁, S₂, S₃ ∈ P(D): (S₁ ⊆ S₂ ∧ S₂ ⊆ S₃) → S₁ ⊆ S₃
   (Subset relation chains)

ALSO STATE:
4. JOIN EXISTENCE: ∀ S₁, S₂ ∈ P(D): S₁ ∪ S₂ ∈ P(D) ∧ S₁ ∪ S₂ = lub(S₁, S₂)
   (Union is the least upper bound)

WELL-DEFINEDNESS OBLIGATION:
5. DATOM EQUALITY: Define when two datoms d₁, d₂ are equal. d₁ = d₂ iff d₁.e = d₂.e ∧ d₁.a = d₂.a ∧ d₁.v = d₂.v ∧ d₁.tx = d₂.tx ∧ d₁.op = d₂.op. This is structural equality, independent of BLAKE3 hash.

BLAKE3 COLLISION BOUND:
6. For 2^64 datoms (far beyond any practical store), BLAKE3 collision probability < 2^{-128}. For practical store sizes (< 2^32 datoms), collision probability is negligible.

DELIVERABLE: Formal statement as a LaTeX-like proof outline in spec/07-merge.md or a dedicated spec/formal-proofs.md.

title: R2.1b: Write proofs for all 4 partial order properties + join existence
description:
Write the actual proofs. These are standard set-theoretic proofs that should be straightforward:

PROOF 1 (Reflexivity): For any S ∈ P(D), every element x ∈ S is also in S (trivially). Therefore S ⊆ S. ∎

PROOF 2 (Antisymmetry): Assume S₁ ⊆ S₂ and S₂ ⊆ S₁. For any x: x ∈ S₁ → x ∈ S₂ (from S₁ ⊆ S₂) and x ∈ S₂ → x ∈ S₁ (from S₂ ⊆ S₁). Therefore S₁ = S₂ by extensionality. ∎

PROOF 3 (Transitivity): Assume S₁ ⊆ S₂ and S₂ ⊆ S₃. For any x ∈ S₁: x ∈ S₂ (from S₁ ⊆ S₂), then x ∈ S₃ (from S₂ ⊆ S₃). Therefore S₁ ⊆ S₃. ∎

PROOF 4 (Join = LUB): S₁ ∪ S₂ is an upper bound: S₁ ⊆ S₁ ∪ S₂ and S₂ ⊆ S₁ ∪ S₂ (by definition of union). It is least: for any U with S₁ ⊆ U and S₂ ⊆ U, every x ∈ S₁ ∪ S₂ is in S₁ or S₂, hence in U. So S₁ ∪ S₂ ⊆ U. ∎

COROLLARY: (P(D), ⊆, ∪) is a join-semilattice. ∎

ALSO PROVE:
- Content-addressable identity preserves set semantics: if hash(d₁) = hash(d₂) then d₁ = d₂ (with negligible collision probability). This means deduplication by hash is correct.

DEPENDS ON: R2.1a (formal statement).
DELIVERABLE: Complete proofs in spec.
QUALITY CRITERION: Each proof is self-contained and mechanically verifiable.
close_reason: Covered by R2.1a agent — all 4 partial order proofs + join existence already written in spec/01-store.md

title: R2.2: EXPLORE — Cascade-as-fixpoint to restore L1/L2 commutativity/associativity
description:
NEEDS FIRST-PRINCIPLES EXPLORATION via 2 Opus 4.6 subagents.

WHAT: merge(A, B) triggers cascading retractions that depend on order of evaluation. This means merge(A, B) ≠ merge(B, A) when cascades are involved, breaking L1 (commutativity) and L2 (associativity) — the two most critical CRDT properties.

PROPOSED FIX: Separate merge from cascade. Define:
- merge(A, B) = A ∪ B (pure set union — provably commutative and associative)
- cascade(S) = deterministic fixpoint computation on a merged store S

Then the full operation is: cascade(merge(A, B)). If cascade is deterministic (same input → same output), then the composition is commutative: cascade(A ∪ B) = cascade(B ∪ A).

QUESTIONS FOR EXPLORATION:
1. Is cascade actually deterministic? What ordering does it use internally?
2. Can cascade be expressed as a monotone function on a lattice? (This would guarantee fixpoint existence via Knaster-Tarski.)
3. What is the cascade evaluation order? Per-attribute? Per-entity? Topological?
4. Are there cyclic cascades? If so, how are they handled?
5. Can we prove cascade terminates? (Bounded store + monotone → termination.)

DELIVERABLE: Formal cascade specification. Proof that cascade(merge(A,B)) is commutative and associative. Add to spec/07-merge.md or spec/04-resolution.md.

NOTE: This is deferred to V2_AUDIT for implementation, but the formal specification and proof must be completed NOW to ensure the algebra is sound before implementation begins.

SOURCES: Agent 3, Agent 10.
TRACES: spec/07-merge.md INV-MERGE-001 (commutativity), INV-MERGE-002 (associativity), spec/04-resolution.md.
close_reason: R2.2: Resolution-Merge composition proof added to spec/04-resolution.md §4.3.1. Proves LIVE is a derived CRDT for all 3 resolution modes.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: Cascade-as-fixpoint is the most critical CRDT fix. If cascade is NOT deterministic, the entire CRDT algebra breaks down. Key insight: cascade must be a monotone function on a lattice (Knaster-Tarski guarantees fixpoint existence). The evaluation order must be deterministic — suggest topological order over entity-attribute pairs, with stable sorting for ties.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: Cascade-as-fixpoint addresses a specific audit finding: the current spec describes multi-attribute resolution as sequential cascading, but commutativity and associativity are only proven for individual attributes. If resolution of attribute A affects attribute B (e.g., through schema dependencies), the cascade order matters — violating CRDT guarantees. The proposed fix: define cascade as a fixpoint computation that iterates until stable. This restores commutativity/associativity at the system level. The exploration must: (1) Define the cascade function formally. (2) Prove fixpoint existence (lattice + monotonicity = Kleene's theorem). (3) Prove fixpoint uniqueness. (4) Show convergence bound.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: EXPLORATION PROTOCOL: Use 2x Opus 4.6 subagents exploring independently. Agent A: one agent defines fixpoint, other verifies convergence. Spec source. Agent B: challenge/verify Agent A's conclusions. Synthesize both results into a reconciliation recommendation. Write findings to V1_AUDIT_TRIAGE.md in the corresponding section.

title: R2.3: EXPLORE — Causal independence via predecessor sets (not HLC)
description:
NEEDS FIRST-PRINCIPLES EXPLORATION via 2 Opus 4.6 subagents.

WHAT: detect_conflicts uses HLC ordering to determine causal independence, but HLC tracks physical time, not causal precedence. Two causally independent events can have ordered HLCs (e.g., agent A and agent B both transact at different wall times but without knowledge of each other). This means the conflict detector can miss true conflicts.

FORMAL PROBLEM: Let tx₁ and tx₂ be transactions. tx₁ ∥ tx₂ (causally independent) iff tx₁ ∉ causal_predecessors(tx₂) ∧ tx₂ ∉ causal_predecessors(tx₁). But HLC ordering gives: hlc(tx₁) < hlc(tx₂) does NOT imply tx₁ → tx₂ (causally precedes). HLC only guarantees: tx₁ → tx₂ implies hlc(tx₁) < hlc(tx₂) (one direction).

FIX: Use explicit causal predecessor sets (already defined in Transaction type) instead of HLC comparison for independence detection. HLC is still useful for total ordering (tie-breaking), but NOT for causal reasoning.

QUESTIONS:
1. Does the spec conflate HLC ordering with causal ordering anywhere else?
2. What is the causal predecessor representation? Set of TxIds? Vector clock?
3. How does the frontier map relate to causal predecessors?
4. Can we prove that causal_predecessors correctly captures the happens-before relation?

DELIVERABLE: Updated conflict detection specification using causal predecessors. Formal proof of correctness. Update spec/04-resolution.md and spec/07-merge.md.

SOURCES: Agent 3, Agent 10.
TRACES: spec/04-resolution.md, spec/07-merge.md, SEED.md §4 (HLC), PD-001 (agent working set).
close_reason: R2.3: INV-STORE-010 rewritten to distinguish causal order (predecessor sets) from HLC order. Added causally_independent() implementation.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: EXPLORATION PROTOCOL: Use 2x Opus 4.6 subagents exploring independently. Agent A: one agent formalizes predecessor sets, other tests HLC insufficiency. Spec. Agent B: challenge/verify Agent A's conclusions. Synthesize both results into a reconciliation recommendation. Write findings to V1_AUDIT_TRIAGE.md in the corresponding section.

title: R2.4: EXPLORE — Semilattice witness requirement for user-defined lattice attributes
description:
NEEDS FIRST-PRINCIPLES EXPLORATION via 2 Opus 4.6 subagents.

WHAT: INV-RESOLUTION-002 allows lattice-resolved attributes with user-supplied join() functions, but there is NO validation that user-supplied join() actually forms a valid semilattice. A buggy join() that violates associativity, commutativity, or idempotency would silently corrupt the CRDT merge.

FORMAL REQUIREMENT: For any user-defined lattice type L with join operation ⊔:
1. Associativity: ∀ a, b, c ∈ L: (a ⊔ b) ⊔ c = a ⊔ (b ⊔ c)
2. Commutativity: ∀ a, b ∈ L: a ⊔ b = b ⊔ a
3. Idempotency: ∀ a ∈ L: a ⊔ a = a

PROPOSED SOLUTION: Require a 'semilattice witness' at schema definition time:
- Property-based test: proptest with random inputs verifying all 3 laws
- Or: proof obligation that the implementing agent must discharge
- Or: restrict to known-safe lattice types (counters, max, min, sets, LWW)

QUESTIONS:
1. Should we restrict lattice types at Stage 0 to built-in types only (LWW, Multi, known lattices)?
2. Is runtime validation (quickcheck at schema registration) sufficient, or do we need compile-time guarantees?
3. What is the error recovery if a lattice violation is detected post-merge?
4. Can we use Rust trait bounds (Semilattice trait with laws) to provide compile-time guarantees?

DELIVERABLE: Updated INV-RESOLUTION-002 with validation requirement. Semilattice trait definition. Stage 0 may restrict to built-in lattices with user-defined deferred.

SOURCES: Agent 3, Agent 10.
TRACES: spec/04-resolution.md INV-RESOLUTION-002, SEED.md §4 (per-attribute resolution).
close_reason: R2.4: INV-SCHEMA-007 strengthened with 6-property semilattice witness verification. V:KANI added. verify_semilattice() impl contract.

Agent B: challenge/verify Agent A's conclusions. Synthesize both results into a reconciliation recommendation. Write findings to V1_AUDIT_TRIAGE.md in the corresponding section.

title: R2.5: Prove 5 UNPROVEN CRDT properties — formal methods pass 1
description:
Formally prove the 5 CRDT properties currently classified as CORRECT-BUT-UNPROVEN:

1. Join-semilattice: (P(D), ⊆, ∪) forms a join-semilattice (depends on R2.1)
2. LWW semilattice: LWW resolution with total ordering forms a valid semilattice
3. Content-addressable identity: BLAKE3 hash produces unique identifiers for distinct datoms (collision bound analysis)
4. Conservative conflict detection: All true conflicts are detected (may over-report, never under-report)
5. At-least-once delivery idempotence: Re-delivering the same datoms has no effect (follows from set union idempotency)

APPROACH: For each property:
1. State the property formally (first-order logic or set theory)
2. Write the proof (constructive where possible)
3. Identify assumptions and axioms used
4. Create proptest harness for empirical validation
5. If TLA+ feasible, encode the property

DEPENDS ON: R2.1 (partial order proof), R2.3 (causal independence — affects conservative detection).

DELIVERABLE: Formal proofs added to spec. Proptest harnesses specified in guide.

SOURCES: Agent 10 (CRDT Algebraic Correctness).
TRACES: spec/07-merge.md, SEED.md §4.
close_reason: R2.5: All CRDT properties proven — G-Set, commutativity, associativity, idempotency, LWW semilattice, conservative detection, composition.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: CONTAINER: Work the subtasks, not this parent. Close when all subtasks are done.

title: R2.5a: Prove LWW semilattice property (join operation well-defined)
description:
PROVE: LWW resolution with BLAKE3 tie-breaking forms a valid semilattice.

FORMAL SETUP: For attribute a with ResolutionMode::Lww, define:
- Values: the set of all (value, txid) pairs asserted for [e, a]
- Join: lww_join(v₁, v₂) = the (value, txid) pair with higher HLC timestamp, breaking ties by BLAKE3 hash of the containing datom

PROOF OBLIGATIONS:
1. Commutativity: lww_join(v₁, v₂) = lww_join(v₂, v₁) — follows from total order on (HLC, BLAKE3)
2. Associativity: lww_join(lww_join(v₁, v₂), v₃) = lww_join(v₁, lww_join(v₂, v₃)) — follows from transitivity of total order
3. Idempotency: lww_join(v, v) = v — trivial

KEY INSIGHT: LWW semilattice correctness depends on (HLC timestamp, BLAKE3 hash) forming a total order. If HLC provides a total order, ties are broken by BLAKE3 (also total since it's a hash). The composition of two total orders with lexicographic comparison is a total order.

SUBTLETY: HLC is NOT a total order in general (two agents can have identical timestamps). After R0.1 (BLAKE3 tie-breaking), the combined key (HLC, BLAKE3) IS total. This proof DEPENDS ON R0.1 being applied first.

DEPENDS ON: R0.1 (BLAKE3 tie-breaking ADR), R2.1 (base semilattice proof).
DELIVERABLE: Formal proof added to spec/04-resolution.md or spec/formal-proofs.md.
close_reason: Covered by R2.2 agent — LWW semilattice proved in resolution-merge composition proof §4.3.1

title: R2.5b: Prove conservative conflict detection completeness
description:
PROVE: Conflict detection is conservative — it detects all true conflicts (may over-report, never under-report).

FORMAL SETUP:
- Conflict: Two datoms d₁, d₂ assert different values for the same [entity, attribute] from causally independent transactions.
- Causal independence: tx₁ ∥ tx₂ iff tx₁ ∉ causal_predecessors(tx₂) ∧ tx₂ ∉ causal_predecessors(tx₁)
- Conservative: ∀ true conflicts C: detect(C) = true. (No false negatives.)

PROOF STRATEGY:
1. Define 'true conflict' formally using causal independence (from R2.3)
2. Show that the detection algorithm checks all pairs of datoms for same [e, a]
3. Show that detection uses causal_predecessors (not HLC) for independence
4. Prove: if d₁ and d₂ conflict, the detection algorithm will find them

SUBTLETY: This proof depends on R2.3 (causal independence fix). With HLC-based detection, the proof FAILS because HLC-ordered but causally independent transactions can be missed. With predecessor-based detection, the proof holds.

DEPENDS ON: R2.3 (causal independence via predecessor sets).
DELIVERABLE: Formal proof added to spec.
close_reason: R2.5b: Conservative conflict detection proof in spec/04-resolution.md §4.3.2. No false negatives proven via contrapositive.

title: R2.5c: Design proptest harnesses for all proven CRDT properties
description:
For every CRDT property proven in R2.1, R2.5a, R2.5b, design the corresponding proptest harness that will validate the proof empirically at implementation time.

HARNESSES NEEDED:
1. Partial order reflexivity: arb_store() |s| assert s ⊆ s
2. Partial order antisymmetry: arb_store_pair() |s1, s2| if s1 ⊆ s2 && s2 ⊆ s1 then s1 == s2
3. Partial order transitivity: arb_store_triple() |s1, s2, s3| if s1 ⊆ s2 && s2 ⊆ s3 then s1 ⊆ s3
4. Join = LUB: arb_store_pair() |s1, s2| s1 ⊆ merge(s1, s2) && s2 ⊆ merge(s1, s2)
5. Commutativity (L1): arb_store_pair() |s1, s2| merge(s1, s2) == merge(s2, s1)
6. Associativity (L2): arb_store_triple() |s1, s2, s3| merge(merge(s1, s2), s3) == merge(s1, merge(s2, s3))
7. Idempotency (L3): arb_store() |s| merge(s, s) == s
8. Monotonicity (L4): arb_store_pair() |s1, s2| s1 ⊆ merge(s1, s2) (datoms only grow)
9. LWW commutativity: arb_lww_pair() |v1, v2| lww_join(v1, v2) == lww_join(v2, v1)
10. LWW associativity: arb_lww_triple() |v1, v2, v3| ...
11. Conservative detection: arb_conflicting_stores() |s1, s2| all_true_conflicts_detected(s1, s2)

NOTE: Harnesses 5 and 6 need the cascade-as-fixpoint fix (R2.2) to pass. Until then, they test merge WITHOUT cascade.

DELIVERABLE: Proptest harness specifications added to guide verification section. These become the test suite the implementing agent writes.

QUALITY CRITERION: Every proven property has a corresponding proptest harness.
close_reason: R2.5c: CRDT verification suite added to guide/10-verification.md §10.7. 24 proptests, 8 harnesses, 16 INVs covered.

title: R2.6: Write TLA+ specification for CRDT merge algebra
description:
Create a TLA+ specification encoding the CRDT merge algebra for model checking.

SCOPE: Encode:
1. Store state (set of datoms)
2. Merge operation (set union)
3. CRDT laws (L1-L5: commutativity, associativity, idempotency, identity, monotonicity)
4. Cascade as deterministic fixpoint (from R2.2)
5. Conflict detection (from R2.3)
6. Resolution modes (LWW, Multi, Lattice)

PROPERTIES TO CHECK:
- Safety: merge never loses datoms (monotonicity)
- Liveness: cascade always terminates
- Commutativity: merge(A,B) = merge(B,A)
- Associativity: merge(merge(A,B),C) = merge(A,merge(B,C))
- Convergence: all replicas that have seen the same set of transactions have identical store state

DEPENDS ON: R2.1 (partial order), R2.2 (cascade fixpoint), R2.3 (causal independence), R2.5 (UNPROVEN proofs).

DELIVERABLE: TLA+ spec file. Model checking results. Added to spec/ as formal verification artifact.

SOURCES: Agent 10, Agent 13 (Verification Pipeline).
TRACES: SEED.md §4, spec/07-merge.md.
close_reason: R2.6: TLA+ spec written (468 lines) + companion guide (370 lines). 11 properties modeled across safety/temporal/liveness. audits/stage-0/research/braid-crdt.tla

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: TLA+ specification for the CRDT merge algebra. This creates a formal model that can be checked by TLC model checker. The spec encodes: (1) Store as a set of datoms. (2) Merge as set union. (3) Resolution modes (LWW, lattice, multi-value) as functions over attribute values. (4) Cascade as fixpoint. (5) Safety properties (commutativity, associativity, idempotence, monotonicity). (6) Liveness property (fixpoint convergence). This is the gold standard for formal verification — it finds counterexamples that proofs might miss. Depends on R2.1-R2.4 CRDT proofs being complete so we know what properties to encode.

title: PageRank Convergence: Perron-Frobenius spectral gap guarantees
description:
# PageRank Convergence via Perron-Frobenius

## Task
Implement PageRank with formal convergence guarantees from spectral theory.

## Algorithm
PR(v) = (1-d)/|V| + d * sum(PR(u)/out_degree(u) for u in in_neighbors(v))
Power iteration: x_{k+1} = M * x_k where M = (1-d)/|V| * J + d * P
- d = damping factor (default 0.85)
- P = column-stochastic transition matrix
- J = all-ones matrix (teleportation)

## Perron-Frobenius Application
M is a positive stochastic matrix (all entries > 0 due to teleportation).
By Perron-Frobenius:
- Dominant eigenvalue lambda_1 = 1 (since M is stochastic)
- Dominant eigenvector has all positive entries (the PageRank vector)
- Second eigenvalue |lambda_2| <= d (the damping factor)
- Convergence rate: O(d^k) — geometric convergence

## Spectral Gap
gap = 1 - |lambda_2| >= 1 - d
Larger gap = faster convergence. With d=0.85, convergence is O(0.85^k).
After 50 iterations: error < 0.85^50 approx 10^-4.

## Properties (INV-QUERY-014)
1. Normalization: sum of all PageRank scores = 1 (stochastic matrix)
2. Convergence: power iteration converges for connected graphs
3. Monotonicity: adding edges to a node increases its PageRank
4. Determinism: same graph, same parameters = same scores
5. Non-negativity: all scores >= 0 (Perron-Frobenius)

## Verification
- V:PROP — Scores sum to 1 (normalization)
- V:PROP — Convergence within max_iterations for connected graphs
- V:PROP — Adding edge to node v increases PR(v)
- V:PROP — Non-negativity of all scores
- V:KANI — kani_pagerank_normalized, kani_pagerank_nonnegative
- V:KANI — kani_pagerank_monotone_edge_addition

## Spec Reference
spec/03-query.md INV-QUERY-014, lines ~659-710. Stage 0 graph algorithm.
close_reason: 4 proptest proofs: convergence within 100 iter, stochasticity, non-negativity, spectral gap bound d^k

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
[AUDIT-R3 MEDIUM FIX] Kani floating-point limitation workaround.

Kani does NOT support floating-point arithmetic natively. For PageRank convergence
(which requires FP iteration), use the following verification strategy:

1. V:KANI — Use FIXED-POINT ARITHMETIC (rational numbers as i64 numerator/denominator)
   for bounded model checking. Verify convergence of the power iteration in rational
   arithmetic for graphs with ≤ 8 nodes.

2. V:PROP — Use proptest with f64 arithmetic for statistical verification.
   Generate random graphs (1-100 nodes), run PageRank, verify:
   - Sum of ranks = 1.0 (within epsilon)
   - Convergence in ≤ 100 iterations for damping factor d = 0.85
   - Rank vector is the Perron eigenvector (verify via power method residual < 1e-10)

3. ANALYTICAL PROOF (in proof bead, not code):
   The Perron-Frobenius theorem guarantees convergence for the Google matrix
   G = (1-d)/n * J + d * M (where M is column-stochastic, J is all-ones).
   G is primitive (positive entries) ⇒ unique dominant eigenvalue λ₁ = 1.
   Rate of convergence: |λ₂/λ₁| ≤ d = 0.85.
   After k iterations: ||π_k - π*||₁ ≤ 2 * d^k.
   For k = 65 iterations: 2 * 0.85^65 < 1e-4 (sufficient precision).

V:KANI — rational arithmetic version converges for |V| ≤ 8
V:PROP — f64 version converges for random graphs, sum(ranks) ≈ 1.0

title: Implement ToolResponse output algebra and OutputMode dispatch
description:
# ToolResponse Output Algebra (spec/14-interface.md §14.4, INV-INTERFACE-001)

## Task
Implement ToolResponse as an algebraic type with OutputMode dispatch.

## ToolResponse Type
```rust
pub struct ToolResponse {
    pub content: ResponseContent,
    pub guidance: GuidanceFooter,
    pub metadata: ResponseMetadata,
}

pub enum ResponseContent {
    Datoms(Vec<Datom>),
    QueryResult(QueryResult),
    Status(StoreStatus),
    HarvestResult(HarvestResult),
    SeedOutput(SeedOutput),
    GuidanceState(GuidanceState),
    TransactReceipt(TxReceipt),
    Error(BraidError),
}

pub struct ResponseMetadata {
    pub command: String,
    pub duration_ms: u64,
    pub datom_count: usize,
    pub token_estimate: usize,
}
```

## OutputMode (INV-INTERFACE-001)
```rust
pub enum OutputMode {
    Text,  // human-readable, with guidance footer
    Json,  // JSON, with guidance in metadata
    Edn,   // EDN canonical form
}
```

## Output Algebra (INV-INTERFACE-001)
The output algebra ensures EVERY response follows the same structure:
response = content + guidance + metadata

This is an algebra because:
- composition: responses can be composed (batch operations)
- identity: empty response (no datoms, no guidance)
- associativity: (a ∘ b) ∘ c = a ∘ (b ∘ c) for batch composition

## Semantic Equivalence (INV-INTERFACE-010)
For any input I and command C:
  CLI(C, I) ≡ MCP(C, I)
Same content, same metadata, same guidance. Different serialization only.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] ToolResponse type with content + guidance + metadata
- [ ] ResponseContent covers all command outputs
- [ ] OutputMode dispatches to text/json/edn serializers
- [ ] Every response includes guidance footer (INV-GUIDANCE-001)
- [ ] CLI and MCP produce equivalent ToolResponses

## Traces To
spec/14-interface.md §14.4, guide/09-interface.md §9.2, INV-INTERFACE-001, INV-INTERFACE-010
close_reason: output.rs: OutputMode enum (Json, Agent, Human)

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
[AUDIT-R3 CRITICAL FIX] OutputMode enum correction:

WRONG (current description says): Text, Json, Edn
CORRECT (per spec/14-interface.md §14.3): Json, Agent, Human

The three modes per INV-INTERFACE-001 are:
  - Json: structured output for programmatic consumption
  - Agent: LLM-optimized output with guidance footers, token-budget awareness
  - Human: terminal-formatted output with colors, tables, progress bars

Each mode must implement the OutputSurface trait:
  fn format_datoms(&self, datoms: &[Datom], budget: TokenBudget) -> FormattedOutput;
  fn format_error(&self, err: &BraidError) -> FormattedOutput;
  fn format_guidance(&self, g: &GuidancePayload) -> FormattedOutput;

Agent mode is the PRIMARY innovation — it is the LLM-native output surface that makes every CLI response a prompt-engineered artifact. Human mode is secondary. There is no 'Text' or 'Edn' mode at Stage 0.

V:TYPE — enum variants compile with exhaustive match
V:PROP — each mode produces valid FormattedOutput for all input types

title: Causal Order Partial Order: happens-before DAG and HLC consistency
description:
# Causal Order Partial Order

## Task
Implement and verify the causal ordering structure on transactions, which is a SEPARATE
partial order from the store subset ordering.

## Two Distinct Partial Orders in the System
1. (P(D), subset) — the store semilattice (PO-1/2/3)
2. (Transactions, <_causal) — the happens-before partial order (this bead)

## Happens-Before Definition
tx1 <_causal tx2 iff tx2.predecessors contains tx1 (transitively).
This is the transitive closure of the explicit predecessor relation.
NOT defined by HLC ordering — HLC is merely consistent with causality.

## Properties
- Irreflexivity: NOT (tx <_causal tx) — no transaction causes itself
- Transitivity: tx1 <_causal tx2 and tx2 <_causal tx3 implies tx1 <_causal tx3
- Asymmetry: tx1 <_causal tx2 implies NOT (tx2 <_causal tx1)
- The predecessor graph forms a DAG (directed acyclic graph)

## Causal Independence (Concurrency)
tx1 || tx2 iff NOT (tx1 <_causal tx2) AND NOT (tx2 <_causal tx1)
- Concurrency is symmetric: tx1 || tx2 iff tx2 || tx1
- Concurrent transactions are the source of conflicts

## HLC Consistency
HLC respects causality: tx1 <_causal tx2 implies hlc(tx1) < hlc(tx2)
But NOT vice versa: hlc(tx1) < hlc(tx2) does NOT imply tx1 <_causal tx2
HLC is a MONOTONE MAP from (Transactions, <_causal) to (Timestamps, <)
but not an ORDER EMBEDDING (not injective on the order).

## Vector Clock Frontier
Per-agent vector clock where merge is pointwise max.
Forms its own join-semilattice: (VectorClock, pointwise_max).
Commutativity, associativity, idempotency of pointwise max.

## Verification
- V:PROP — Predecessor graph is acyclic (no cycles in causal order)
- V:PROP — HLC consistency: causal implies HLC ordering
- V:PROP — Concurrency symmetry: tx1 || tx2 iff tx2 || tx1
- V:KANI — kani_causal_dag (no cycles), kani_hlc_causal_consistent
- V:KANI — kani_vector_clock_semilattice (pointwise max laws)

## Spec Reference
spec/01-store.md INV-STORE-010, ADR-STORE-021. Traces to SEED.md S4.
close_reason: 3 proptest proofs: irreflexivity, DAG acyclicity, HLC consistency with causal order

title: Implement Schema Layer 2: Specification Element attributes (40 attributes)
description:
# Schema Layer 2: Specification Element Attributes

## Task
Register the ~40 Layer 2 attributes needed for DDIS specification elements.

## Attribute Groups

### Identity (4 attributes)
:spec/id         — String  :one   — INV-STORE-001, ADR-SCHEMA-002, etc.
:spec/type       — Keyword :one   — :invariant, :adr, :negative-case, :uncertainty
:spec/namespace  — Keyword :one   — :store, :schema, :query, etc.
:spec/statement  — String  :one   — the invariant/ADR statement text

### Traceability (5 attributes)
:spec/traces-to    — String :many  — SEED.md section references
:spec/depends-on   — Ref    :many  — other spec elements this depends on
:spec/affects       — Ref    :many  — spec elements this modifies
:spec/constrains    — Ref    :many  — spec elements this bounds
:spec/tests         — Ref    :many  — verification elements for this

### Verification (6 attributes)
:spec/falsification — String :one   — falsification condition
:spec/verification  — Keyword :many — V:TYPE, V:PROP, V:KANI, V:MODEL
:spec/stage         — Long   :one   — implementation stage (0, 1, 2, ...)
:spec/witnessed     — Boolean :one  — has test evidence
:spec/challenged    — Boolean :one  — has been challenged
:spec/proptest      — String :one   — proptest function name

### ADR-Specific (4 attributes)
:spec/adr-problem     — String :one — problem statement
:spec/adr-options     — String :many — options considered
:spec/adr-decision    — String :one — chosen option
:spec/adr-alternatives — String :many — rejected alternatives with rationale

### Negative Cases (2 attributes)
:spec/neg-violation — String :one — what constitutes a violation
:spec/neg-safety    — String :one — safety property (temporal logic)

### Uncertainty (2 attributes)
:spec/confidence    — Double :one — confidence level [0.0, 1.0]
:spec/resolves-when — String :one — what would resolve this uncertainty

### Trilateral (14 attributes)
Intent (7): :intent/noted, :intent/decision, :intent/rationale,
           :intent/source, :intent/goal, :intent/constraint, :intent/preference
Implementation (5): :impl/module, :impl/file, :impl/signature,
                    :impl/implements, :impl/test-result
Cross-Boundary (2): :spec/implements, :spec/level

## Layer Dependency Validation
All L2 attributes reference only L0 types (String, Keyword, Ref, Long, Double, Boolean).
This satisfies INV-SCHEMA-004 (layer dependency rule).

## Registration as Transaction
All 40 attributes registered in a single post-genesis transaction with:
- :tx/provenance = :bootstrap
- :tx/source = :schema-registration
- Each attribute gets :db/layerIndex = 2

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All ~40 L2 attributes registered
- [ ] Layer dependency rule satisfied (no L3+ references)
- [ ] Self-bootstrap (C7): spec elements use these attrs to describe themselves
- [ ] Query: all L2 attributes returns ~40 results

## Traces To
spec/02-schema.md §2.4-2.5, guide/02-schema.md, INV-SCHEMA-006
close_reason: 60 domain attributes (24 L1 trilateral + 36 L2 spec-element), 18 schema tests, layer_1/layer_2 API

title: [P3] Persistent homology over transaction filtration
Implement AssembledContext and SchemaNeighborhood types for SEED
description:
# AssembledContext and SchemaNeighborhood Types (guide/06-seed.md)

## Task
Implement the intermediate types that flow through the SEED pipeline.

## SchemaNeighborhood (ASSOCIATE output)
```rust
pub struct SchemaNeighborhood {
    pub seed_entities: Vec<EntityId>,       // starting points
    pub expanded: Vec<(EntityId, f64)>,     // (entity, relevance) pairs
    pub depth_reached: u32,                  // actual expansion depth
    pub breadth_reached: u32,                // actual expansion breadth
    pub boundary: Vec<EntityId>,             // entities at expansion boundary
}
```
This is the output of ASSOCIATE (INV-SEED-003) — bounded by depth*breadth.

## AssembledContext (ASSEMBLE output, COMPRESS input)
```rust
pub struct AssembledContext {
    pub seed_output: SeedOutput,          // the five-part structure
    pub token_estimate: usize,            // estimated token count
    pub projection_map: HashMap<EntityId, ProjectionLevel>,  // per-entity projection
    pub dropped: Vec<(EntityId, String)>,  // entities dropped with reason
}
```

## Pipeline Flow
```
Store → ASSOCIATE → SchemaNeighborhood → ASSEMBLE → AssembledContext → COMPRESS → SeedOutput
```

## INV-SEED-003 Enforcement
SchemaNeighborhood.expanded.len() <= depth * breadth (default 3*10 = 30).
If the graph is larger, the boundary records what was NOT expanded.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SchemaNeighborhood type with bounded expansion tracking
- [ ] AssembledContext type with token estimation
- [ ] Pipeline types compose: ASSOCIATE→ASSEMBLE→COMPRESS
- [ ] INV-SEED-003: expanded count <= depth * breadth
- [ ] Dropped entities tracked with human-readable reasons

## Traces To

title: Implement LIVE index: resolution-mode-aware current-state view
description:
# LIVE Index: Resolution-Mode-Aware Current State View (INV-STORE-012)

## Task
Implement the LIVE index as a derived view over the raw datom store.

## Separation of Concerns (Critical Architecture Decision)
- Store layer: (P(D), ∪) — pure join-semilattice, NO resolution logic
- LIVE layer: LIVE(S) = fold(causal_sort(S), apply_resolution)

LIVE is a MONOTONE FUNCTION applied AFTER store operations.
S1 ⊆ S2 ⟹ LIVE(S2) reflects all information in LIVE(S1).

## LIVE Computation
```rust
impl Store {
    pub fn live_value(&self, entity: EntityId, attr: &Attribute) -> Option<ResolvedValue> {
        let mode = self.schema().resolution_mode(attr)?;
        let candidates = self.candidates(entity, attr);
        let unretracted = self.net_assert_retract(&candidates);
        Some(mode.resolve(&unretracted))
    }

    pub fn live_entity(&self, entity: EntityId) -> HashMap<Attribute, ResolvedValue> {
        self.schema().attributes()
            .filter_map(|attr| self.live_value(entity, attr).map(|v| (attr.clone(), v)))
            .collect()
    }

    fn candidates(&self, entity: EntityId, attr: &Attribute) -> Vec<&Datom> {
        self.datoms_for(entity, attr)
            .filter(|d| d.op == Op::Assert)
            .collect()
    }

    fn net_assert_retract(&self, candidates: &[&Datom]) -> Vec<&Datom> {
        // For each (entity, attr, value): count asserts and retracts
        // Value is effective iff #asserts > #retracts
        ...
    }
}
```

## ResolvedValue Type
```rust
pub enum ResolvedValue {
    Single(Value),                    // LWW or Lattice result
    Multi(Vec<Value>),                // Multi-value (all unretracted)
    Conflict(Vec<(Value, TxId)>),     // Unresolvable at current frontier
}
```

## Monotonicity Theorem
LIVE is a derived CRDT that inherits strong eventual consistency:
- Store convergence: MERGE is commutative/associative (L1-L2)
- Schema convergence: Schema is datoms, so converges with store
- Resolution convergence: Each mode is a join-semilattice
Therefore: all agents with same datoms compute identical LIVE values.

## LWW Tiebreaker (ADR-RESOLUTION-009)
When HLC timestamps are equal: winner = max by BLAKE3(datom_content).
This ensures total ordering even under timestamp collision.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] LIVE computes per-attribute resolved values
- [ ] Assert/retract netting correct
- [ ] LWW uses HLC ordering with BLAKE3 tiebreaker
- [ ] Lattice mode computes LUB via lattice join
- [ ] MultiValue returns all unretracted values
- [ ] LIVE is monotone: adding datoms never removes resolved values
- [ ] proptest: live_monotonicity, live_resolution_commutativity

## Traces To
spec/01-store.md INV-STORE-012, spec/04-resolution.md, guide/04-resolution.md

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: V:TYPE - Store must expose live_view() returning LiveIndex. LiveIndex wraps BTreeMap<(EntityId,Attribute),ResolvedValue>. V:PROP - Monotonicity: S1 subset S2 implies live(S1) subset-eq live(S2) modulo resolution. Idempotence: live(live_datoms(S)) = live(S). V:KANI - kani_live_lww_deterministic (LWW picks same winner given same inputs), kani_live_multivalue_monotone (adding datom never removes from MV set). ALGEBRAIC: LIVE is a join-semilattice homomorphism from (P(D),union) to (LiveIndex,merge).

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
[AUDIT-R3 MEDIUM FIX] LIVE index specification alignment.

Per spec/01-store.md §1.5.3, LIVE is a DERIVED VIEW, not a separate data structure.
LIVE(S, a) = resolve(mode(a), candidates(S, e, a)) for each (e, a).

LIVE is NOT stored — it is computed on query:
  pub fn live_value(store: &Store, entity: EntityId, attr: Keyword) -> Option<Value> {
      let mode = store.schema().resolution_mode(attr);
      let candidates: Vec<&Datom> = store.eavt_range(entity, attr)
          .filter(|d| d.op == Op::Assert && \!is_retracted(store, d))
          .collect();
      match mode {
          ResolutionMode::LastWriterWins => lww_resolve(&candidates),
          ResolutionMode::Lattice => lattice_resolve(&candidates, store.schema()),
          ResolutionMode::MultiValue => multi_resolve(&candidates),
      }
  }

The 'LIVE index' is a LOGICAL concept — it's the composition of:
  1. Store indexes (EAVT/AEVT/AVET/VAET) for candidate retrieval
  2. Resolution modes for winner selection
  3. Retraction filtering

There is no separate BTreeMap for LIVE. It is a query pattern.

MONOTONICITY: LIVE is a monotone function of the store:
  S₁ ⊆ S₂ ⇒ LIVE(S₂) 'refines' LIVE(S₁)
  (new datoms may change the winner but the computation is deterministic)

V:TYPE — live_value returns Option<Value> (None if no assertions exist)
V:PROP — live_value is deterministic: same store state → same result

title: Conservative Detection Completeness Proof with Causal Path Monotonicity Lemma
description:
# Conservative Detection Completeness Proof (R2.5b)

## Task
Implement and verify the formal proof that local conflict detection has no false negatives.

## Theorem
conflicts_detected(F_local) supseteq conflicts_detected(F_global)
A local frontier always detects at least as many conflicts as the global view.

## Proof Structure
- Definition 1: Causal independence (no causal path between tx1 and tx2)
- Definition 2: Conflict predicate (6 conditions)
- Definition 3: Detected conflict set as function of frontier
- Contrapositive proof in two cases
- Lemma: Causal Path Monotonicity — ordering_F subset ordering_causal for F subset S
- Corollary: Anti-Monotonicity — larger frontiers detect fewer conflicts

## Category-Theoretic Insight
The map F -> conflicts_detected(F) is a CONTRAVARIANT FUNCTOR:
  From (Frontiers, subset) to (P(Conflicts), supseteq)
This reversal of ordering (anti-monotonicity) is a formal order-theoretic duality.

## Verification
- V:PROP — For random F1 subset F2: conflicts(F1) supseteq conflicts(F2)
- V:PROP — Causal path monotonicity: ordering_F refines ordering_causal
- V:KANI — kani_detection_no_false_negatives (local detects superset of global)
- V:KANI — kani_causal_path_monotone (frontier subset preserves causal ordering)

## Spec Reference
spec/04-resolution.md Section 4.3.2. Traces to SEED.md S4.
close_reason: harvest.rs: conservative detection, monotonic store growth verified

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: SCHEDULING: This formal proof bead is intentionally free-standing (no blockers). It should be implemented IN PARALLEL with the corresponding namespace tasks. The proofs verify properties of the implementation, so they must be written alongside the code. They block Verification Infrastructure (brai-2mh7.5) to ensure all proofs pass before CI gates are finalized.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
[AUDIT-R3 MEDIUM FIX] Conservative detection — proof sketch refinement.

THEOREM (R2.5b, Conservative Detection Completeness):
  If a true conflict exists between datoms d₁, d₂ (all 6 conditions hold),
  then ConflictPredicate::is_conflict(d₁, d₂, schema) returns true.

PROOF (by exhaustive case analysis on the 6 conditions):
  Assume d₁, d₂ are a true conflict. Then by definition:
    C1: d₁.entity == d₂.entity ✓ (checked by == on EntityId)
    C2: d₁.attribute == d₂.attribute ✓ (checked by == on Keyword)
    C3: d₁.value \!= d₂.value ✓ (checked by \!= on Value)
    C4: d₁.tx \!= d₂.tx ✓ (checked by \!= on TxId)
    C5: d₁.op == Assert ∧ d₂.op == Assert ✓ (checked by == on Op)
    C6: both in current view ✓ (checked by querying for subsequent retractions)

  Each condition is a decidable predicate on finite data.
  The conjunction of all 6 is computed exactly. QED.

CONSERVATIVENESS: The predicate may report conflicts that are NOT true conflicts
  in the user's intent (e.g., two agents asserting different values for the same
  attribute where one is 'more correct'). This is conservative: false positives
  are possible, false negatives are not. Resolution disambiguates.

CAUSAL PATH MONOTONICITY LEMMA:
  If d₁ ≤_c d₂ (d₁ causally before d₂), then d₁ and d₂ cannot be in conflict.
  PROOF: If d₁ ≤_c d₂, then d₂'s agent knew about d₁ when creating d₂.
  Therefore d₂ is a deliberate update, not a conflict. In LWW mode, d₂ wins.
  In Lattice mode, d₂.value ⊔ d₁.value = d₂.value (since d₂ incorporated d₁).

V:PROP — no false negatives: every injected conflict pair is detected
V:KANI — for all datom pairs with |attrs| ≤ 5: is_conflict terminates

title: [SPEC] Topology framework: full promotion from exploration to spec/19-topology.md
description: Full promotion of the execution-topologies exploration (12 docs, ~3,500 lines) into formal DDIS specification. Creates spec/19-topology.md as new namespace with INV-TOPOLOGY-*, ADR-TOPOLOGY-*, NEG-TOPOLOGY-* elements. Covers: T=(G,Phi,Sigma,Pi) definition, 5 coupling mechanisms, CALM-stratified transitions, scaling authority A(d), cold-start bootstrap, F(T) 7-dimension fitness, F_total composition, topology-as-compilation, quadrilateral coherence model. Traces to: SEED.md §6, §10 (Stage 3), ADR-TRILATERAL-004 (N-lateral extensibility).

title: [SPEC] INV-TOPOLOGY-001..003: T=(G,Phi,Sigma,Pi) formal definition and LIVE_T projection
description: Formalize: (1) Topology T=(G,Phi,Sigma,Pi) — agent graph G, merge policy Phi, signal subscriptions Sigma, assignment policy Pi. (2) LIVE_T projection over TOPO_ATTRS. (3) Named topology patterns (mesh, star, pipeline, hierarchy, hybrid). Source: exploration/execution-topologies/03-topology-definition.md. Includes falsification conditions and V:PROP verification.

title: [SPEC] INV-TOPOLOGY-004..005, ADR-TOPOLOGY-001: Five coupling mechanisms with composite signal
description: Formalize: (1) Five coupling mechanisms: task coupling, spec coupling, data coupling, causal coupling, temporal coupling. (2) Composite coupling signal: C(alpha,beta) = sum(w_i * c_i). (3) Learnable weights via harvest feedback. Source: exploration/execution-topologies/02-coupling-model.md.

title: [SPEC] INV-TOPOLOGY-006..009: CALM-stratified topology transition protocol
description: Formalize: (1) Tier M (monotonic) transitions: channel addition, frequency increase, agent addition — no barrier needed. (2) Tier NM (non-monotonic): channel removal, agent removal — sync barrier required. (3) Grace period completeness (no in-flight data lost). (4) Connectivity preservation (agent graph remains connected). (5) Rollback safety (rollback is always monotonic). Source: exploration/execution-topologies/04-transition-protocol.md + 09-invariants-catalog.md (INV-TOPO-TRANS-001..004).

title: [SPEC] ADR-TOPOLOGY-002..003: Scaling authority A(d) = R(1-C)T with earned trust
description: Formalize: (1) Authority function A(d) = R * (1-C) * T — reputation, complexity, trust. (2) Three-tier delegation: autonomous (Tier 0), supervised (Tier 1), human-required (Tier 2). (3) Trust accumulation from successful outcomes. Source: exploration/execution-topologies/05-scaling-authority.md.

title: [SPEC] INV-TOPOLOGY-010..011: Cold-start monotonic relaxation from mesh
description: Formalize: (1) Cold-start defaults to mesh topology (maximum coordination). (2) Monotonic relaxation: only remove channels when evidence supports it. (3) Convergence guarantee: relaxation terminates at optimal topology within finite steps. (4) Asymmetric cost analysis: conflict cost >> merge cost, so bias toward more merging. Source: exploration/execution-topologies/06-cold-start.md + 09-invariants-catalog.md (INV-TOPO-COLD-001..002).

title: [SPEC] INV-TOPOLOGY-012..014: F(T) seven-dimension fitness + F_total composition
description: Formalize: (1) F(T) = sum(w_i * D_i) over 7 dimensions: throughput, conflict rate, staleness, merge overhead, balance, blocking time, knowledge loss. (2) Default weights stored as datoms. (3) Bilateral convergence loop for topology. (4) F_total = lambda*F(S) + (1-lambda)*F(T) composition. (5) Monotonic improvement under bilateral loop (INV-TOPO-FIT-001). (6) Independence of F(T) and F(S) (INV-TOPO-FIT-002). Source: exploration/execution-topologies/07-fitness-function.md.

title: [SPEC] ADR-TOPOLOGY-004: Topology-as-compilation paradigm (AOT+JIT+PGO)
description: Formalize the paradigm shift: spec dependency graph IS a program, optimal topology IS the compiled execution plan. Three-pass compilation: (1) AOT: derive initial topology from spec structure. (2) JIT: adapt at runtime when uncompilable work appears. (3) PGO: refine via bilateral feedback on F(T). This is what differentiates Braid from all other multi-agent frameworks: coordination is derived from structure, not learned from trial and error. Source: exploration/execution-topologies/11-topology-as-compilation.md.

title: [SPEC] Resolve 5 topology open questions: signals, capabilities, cross-project, observability, verification
description: Resolve or formally document as UNC-TOPOLOGY-* the five open questions from exploration/execution-topologies/08-open-questions.md: (1) Signal system integration: how topology signals compose with existing 8 signal types. (2) Agent capability modeling: capability schema, discovery, evolution. (3) Cross-project topology: federated stores with selective merge. (4) Observability: CLI diagnostics, topology visualization. (5) Formal verification: proptest/kani harnesses for topology invariants.

title: [SPEC] Complete quadrilateral in spec/18-trilateral.md: LIVE_T, Phi_total, 4 bilateral loops
description: Complete the quadrilateral extension in spec/18-trilateral.md: (1) Define LIVE_T projection formally (concrete TOPO_ATTRS list). (2) Formalize Phi_total = w_IS*D_IS + w_SP*D_SP + w_TI*D_TI + w_TP*D_TP. (3) Document four bilateral loops: Intent<->Spec (axiological), Spec<->Impl (structural), Impl<->Topology (procedural), Topology<->Intent (strategic). (4) Update INV-TRILATERAL-007 enumeration. Source: exploration/execution-topologies/01-algebraic-foundations.md §7, 07-fitness-function.md §7.3.

title: R1.1: Create types.md — canonical type catalog for all Braid types
description:
Create a single authoritative type catalog (spec/00-types.md or equivalent) that eliminates all ad-hoc type definitions across namespace files.

RATIONALE: A canonical type catalog is the single most impactful change for eliminating spec-guide divergence. It serves as the 'header file' that both spec and guide reference. The implementing agent can use it as a compilation contract.

title: R1.1a: Design types.md structure — categories, schema, cross-reference format
description:

PROPOSED STRUCTURE for spec/00-types.md:

## Per-type entry format:

title: R1.2: EXPLORE — Resolve QueryExpr type divergence (spec enum vs guide flat struct)
description:
NEEDS FIRST-PRINCIPLES EXPLORATION via 2 Opus 4.6 subagents.

WHAT: Spec defines QueryExpr as a 2-variant enum: { Find { variables, clauses }, Pull { pattern, entity } }. Guide defines ParsedQuery as a flat struct: { find_spec, where_clauses, rules, inputs }. Architecture file uses QueryExpr name but guide's fields. These are structurally incompatible.

TRADEOFFS:
- Spec's enum: Distinguishes Find (relational) from Pull (entity-centric) at the type level. More type-safe. Matches Datomic's dual query model. But Pull queries may not be Stage 0.
- Guide's flat struct: Simpler, covers the common case (Find queries). Rules and inputs support richer Datalog. But loses type-level distinction between query kinds.
- Hybrid: QueryExpr enum where Find variant contains guide's flat fields, Pull variant deferred to Stage 1.

ALSO RESOLVE (cascading from this decision):
- Clause variants: spec (Pattern, Frontier, Not, Aggregate, Ffi) vs guide (DataPattern, RuleApplication, NotClause, OrClause, Frontier)
