title: R1.3: EXPLORE — Resolve SeedOutput vs AssembledContext type divergence
description:
NEEDS FIRST-PRINCIPLES EXPLORATION via 2 Opus 4.6 subagents.

WHAT: Three incompatible definitions exist:
1. Spec: AssembledContext { sections: Vec<ContextSection>, total_tokens, budget_remaining, projection_pattern }
2. Guide/06-seed.md: SeedOutput { orientation, decisions, context, warnings, task }

- Guide/06-seed.md: Orientation, Decisions, Context, Warnings, Task
- Guide/00-architecture.md: Orientation, Prior Decisions, Working Context, Warnings, Task

CRITICAL RESEARCH: Seed-as-Prompt optimization (Pattern 2). The seed is the primary LLM-facing surface — its structure should be optimized for LLM consumption using spec-first-design and prompt-optimization techniques. The section names are not arbitrary; they are prompt engineering decisions that affect activation quality.

QUESTIONS: (1) Which structure better activates LLM reasoning? (2) Are named fields (SeedOutput) or generic sections (AssembledContext) better for prompt engineering? (3) What are the optimal section names for LLM activation? (4) Should budget/projection metadata be part of the type or separate?

OUTCOME: Single canonical type + naming convention. Feeds into Pattern 2 (Seed-as-Prompt) research.

title: R1.8: Write ADR — free functions over Store methods for namespace operations
description:
DECISION MADE: Use free functions taking &Store (not Store methods) for all namespace operations.

WHAT: Spec consistently uses store.query(), store.harvest_detect(), store.associate(), store.merge(). Guide consistently uses query(store, ...), harvest_pipeline(store, ...), assemble_seed(store, ...), merge(target, source). This systemic pattern affects 4+ namespaces.

WHY FREE FUNCTIONS: (1) Better modularity — each namespace is a separate module with its own functions. (2) Clearer ownership — Store owns data, modules own operations. (3) Consistent with Rust idioms for non-self-referential operations. (4) Easier testing — functions take &Store, not &mut self. (5) Guide already uses this pattern consistently.

ACTION: (1) Create ADR-ARCH-NNN (or appropriate namespace). (2) Update all spec API surfaces to use free function signatures. (3) Affected namespaces: QUERY, HARVEST, SEED, MERGE, RESOLUTION. (4) Update types.md to reflect function signatures.

SOURCES: All agents, §4 Pattern 1.
TRACES: SEED.md §4, guide/00-architecture.md.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
[AUDIT-R3 MEDIUM FIX] Kani harness design principles.

KANI HARNESS DESIGN RULES:

1. BOUNDED INPUTS: Use kani::any() with assume() to bound the search space.
   Example: let n = kani::any::<usize>(); kani::assume(n <= 10);

2. NO FLOATING POINT: Kani doesn't support FP natively. For PageRank etc.,
   use rational arithmetic (i64 numerator/denominator) for bounded checks.

3. HARNESS NAMING: verify_{invariant_id}_{property}
   Example: verify_store_001_append_only, verify_hlc_monotone

4. UNWIND BOUNDS: Set per-harness, not globally. Some harnesses need deeper
   unwinding (merge) while others are shallow (content-addressable).

5. MODULAR HARNESSES: One harness per invariant. If a harness grows too complex,
   split into multiple harnesses testing orthogonal aspects.

HARNESS INVENTORY (Stage 0):
  STORE: 4 harnesses (append-only, HLC, merge, content-addr)
  SCHEMA: 3 harnesses (genesis, evolution, validation)
  QUERY: 3 harnesses (T_P monotone, fixpoint convergence, classifier)
  RESOLUTION: 3 harnesses (LWW total order, predicate termination, BLAKE3)
  LAYOUT: 3 harnesses (filename injectivity, hash correctness, EDN determinism)
  TOTAL: 16 harnesses

All 16 must pass at the fast tier (unwind ≤ 5) to merge a PR.

V:KANI — all 16 harnesses verify at fast tier

title: Implement INV-QUERY-023: Edge Laplacian Computation
description:
INV-QUERY-023: Edge Laplacian Computation for sheaf-cohomology-based coherence analysis.

Traces to: INV-QUERY-022 (vertex Laplacian), ADR-QUERY-012 (nalgebra),
  exploration/sheaf-coherence/01-hodge-theory.md §1
Verification: V:PROP, V:KANI
Stage: 0

The edge Laplacian L_1 = B_1^T B_1 + B_2 B_2^T operates on the incidence structure
of the spec-implementation-intent graph. It detects topological holes (spec elements
not reachable from goals via tests, or cycles of mutual dependency without grounding).

IMPLEMENTATION:
  pub fn edge_laplacian(graph: &SpecGraph) -> EdgeLaplacian {
      let b1 = boundary_operator_1(graph);  // edges → vertices
      let b2 = boundary_operator_2(graph);  // triangles → edges
      EdgeLaplacian(b1.transpose() * &b1 + &b2 * b2.transpose())
  }

Uses nalgebra for linear algebra (sparse matrices for large graphs).

V:PROP — L_1 is symmetric positive semi-definite
V:KANI — boundary operators compose to zero: B_1 * B_2 = 0 (for |V| ≤ 6)
close_reason: Edge Laplacian (L₁ = B₁ᵀB₁), boundary_operator_1, DenseMatrix, first_betti_number with Jacobi eigendecomposition. 14 new tests (10 unit + 2 proptest + boundary dimensions + transpose involution). INV-QUERY-023/024 both implemented.

title: Implement HarvestResult, HarvestQuality, and pipeline completion types
description:
# Harvest Pipeline Completion Types

## Task
Implement the types that represent harvest pipeline output and quality metrics.

## HarvestResult (pipeline output)
```rust
pub struct HarvestResult {
    pub candidates: Vec<HarvestCandidate>,
    pub accepted: Vec<HarvestCandidate>,
    pub rejected: Vec<(HarvestCandidate, String)>,  // candidate + rejection reason
    pub transactions: Vec<TxReceipt>,                // committed datoms
    pub quality: HarvestQuality,
    pub session_id: Uuid,
}
```

## HarvestQuality (INV-HARVEST-003 metrics)
```rust
pub struct HarvestQuality {
    pub drift_score: u32,          // |Delta(t)| at session end
    pub false_positive_rate: f64,  // |committed then retracted| / |committed|
    pub false_negative_rate: f64,  // |rejected then rediscovered| / |rejected|
    pub coverage: f64,             // |accepted| / |candidates|
    pub quality_band: QualityBand,
}

pub enum QualityBand {
    Excellent,       // 0-2 uncommitted
    MinorDrift,      // 3-5 uncommitted
    SignificantDrift, // 6+ uncommitted
}
```

## ReconciliationType (harvest candidate classification)
```rust
pub enum ReconciliationType {
    Epistemic,       // Knowledge gap (agent knows, store doesn't)
    Structural,      // Implementation diverges from spec
    Consequential,   // Future risk not yet addressed
    Aleatory,        // Agent-agent disagreement
}
```

## Pipeline Signature
```
harvest_pipeline : Store x SessionContext -> HarvestResult
accept_candidate : HarvestCandidate x AgentId -> Transaction<Building>
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] HarvestResult captures full pipeline output
- [ ] HarvestQuality computes FP/FN rates and quality band
- [ ] QualityBand thresholds match spec (0-2, 3-5, 6+)
- [ ] ReconciliationType classifies all harvest candidates
- [ ] drift_score is u32 per spec

## Traces To
spec/05-harvest.md §5.2-5.3, guide/05-harvest.md, INV-HARVEST-003
close_reason: Implemented in Stage 0 — HarvestResult, HarvestQuality, and pipeline completion types in harvest.rs

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: V:TYPE - HarvestResult{candidates,accepted,rejected,transactions,quality,session_id}. HarvestQuality{total_candidates,accepted_count,rejection_rate,novel_datom_count,duplicate_datom_count}. V:PROP - accepted_count + rejected.len() == total_candidates. rejection_rate = rejected.len() as f64 / total_candidates as f64. novel + duplicate = sum of datom counts across accepted candidates. All transactions reference valid TxIds. V:KANI - kani_harvest_partition_complete (every candidate either accepted or rejected, none lost). INFORMATION-THEORETIC: novel_datom_count measures information gain Delta(t) materialized into the store.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
[AUDIT-R3 MEDIUM FIX] HarvestResult type specification.

  pub struct HarvestResult {
      pub gaps_detected: Vec<EpistemicGap>,
      pub datoms_created: Vec<Datom>,    // harvested gaps as datoms
      pub tx_id: Option<TxId>,           // None if no gaps found (no transaction)
      pub quality: HarvestQuality,
      pub session_summary: SessionSummary,
  }

  pub struct HarvestQuality {
      pub recall: f64,     // estimated fraction of true gaps detected (self-assessment)
      pub precision: f64,  // estimated fraction of detected gaps that are real
      pub coverage: f64,   // fraction of session activity covered by harvest
  }

  pub struct SessionSummary {
      pub turns: usize,
      pub transactions: usize,
      pub queries: usize,
      pub entities_touched: usize,
      pub duration: std::time::Duration,
  }

PIPELINE COMPLETION: harvest() returns HarvestResult.
  If gaps_detected is empty → no transaction needed, tx_id = None.
  If gaps_detected is non-empty → creates a harvest transaction, tx_id = Some(...).

The harvest transaction's source is TxSource::Harvest.
All harvested datoms are in a single transaction (atomic harvest).

V:TYPE — HarvestResult compiles
V:PROP — tx_id.is_some() iff datoms_created.len() > 0

title: EPIC: Phase 4 — Guide Coverage Gaps (Missing Decompositions, Proptests, Examples)
description:
## Purpose

Close all gaps where the guide fails to provide implementation guidance for Stage 0 invariants. The guide's job is to give the implementing agent a three-box decomposition (black box contract → state box design → clear box code) for EVERY Stage 0 INV. Missing decompositions mean the implementing agent must design from scratch — which introduces variance and potential spec violations.

## Why Phase 4 Is Last

Phase 4 tasks require Phases 1-3 to be complete because:
1. Stage assignments must be correct (Phase 1) to know which INVs are Stage 0
2. Architecture decisions must be settled (Phase 2) to write correct decompositions
3. Types must be aligned (Phase 3) to write correct proptests

## Scope

- 5 INVs with missing three-box decompositions (RESOLUTION-003, 007; MERGE-002, 006, 007)
- ~20 INVs with missing proptest strategies across 5 namespaces
- Missing braid merge worked example in guide/11-worked-examples.md
- Missing 10 default guidance derivation rules in guide/08-guidance.md
- Missing Kani harness enumeration in guide/10-verification.md (only 16 of 38 listed)
- Missing NEG case coverage in guide files

## Design Philosophy for Decompositions

When writing a three-box decomposition for a missing INV:
1. BLACK BOX: Copy the invariant statement and falsification condition from the spec
2. STATE BOX: Describe the internal state transitions and data structures
3. CLEAR BOX: Write the Rust implementation contract (types, functions, assertions)
4. PROPTEST: Write the property-based test that verifies the invariant

Each decomposition must be SELF-CONTAINED — the implementing agent should be able to implement the INV from the guide alone, without reading the spec file.

## Success Criteria

After all children are closed:
- Every Stage 0 INV has a three-box decomposition in its guide file
- Every Stage 0 INV has a proptest strategy in its guide file
- guide/11-worked-examples.md covers all Stage 0 CLI commands
- guide/10-verification.md enumerates all 38 Kani harnesses
- guide/08-guidance.md enumerates all default derivation rules

## Audit Provenance

Findings from: 14-subagent Fagan inspection audit, 2026-03-03
close_reason: All 22 Phase 4 guide coverage gap beads complete: 4 three-box decompositions, 14 proptests, 38 Kani harness list, 10 derivation rules, NEG coverage, bootstrap path, merge worked example, and misc fixes

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
HOW PHASE 4 SERVES THE ZERO-DEFECT GOAL

Phase 4 closes COVERAGE GAPS — places where the guide does not give the implementing agent enough information to build an invariant correctly. Each gap is a point where the agent must IMPROVISE.

Improvisation is the enemy of zero-defect software. When an agent improvises:
1. It draws on its training data (generic patterns, not project-specific)
2. It may produce code that compiles but violates domain constraints
3. It cannot verify its improvisation against a specification (the specification is the gap)
4. The resulting code is UNTESTABLE against the spec (no proptest, no falsification condition)

Phase 4 eliminates improvisation by providing:
- Three-box decompositions for every Stage 0 INV (no design left to the agent)
- Proptest strategies for every Stage 0 INV (the agent knows exactly what to test)
- Worked examples showing complete workflows (the agent sees the system in action)
- NEG case coverage showing what NOT to build (the agent knows the boundaries)

The shared arb_* generators (brai-39v.20) are particularly important: they ensure all proptests operate on the SAME distribution of test data. Without shared generators, each namespace would define incompatible arb_store() implementations, causing test failures at integration time.

After Phase 4, the guide should be a COMPLETE instruction manual — the implementing agent reads it once and builds the system without needing to read the spec. The spec exists for VERIFICATION, not INSTRUCTION.

title: Add three-box decomposition for INV-RESOLUTION-003 and INV-RESOLUTION-007
description:
## Finding ID: HIGH-5

## Problem

INV-RESOLUTION-003 and INV-RESOLUTION-007 are Stage 0 (confirmed after Stage column fixes) but guide/04-resolution.md provides NO three-box decomposition for either. The guide only decomposes INV-RESOLUTION-001, 002, 004, 005, 006 — skipping 003 and 007 entirely.

## Context

### INV-RESOLUTION-003:
Read spec/05-resolution.md for the invariant statement. This likely covers resolution determinism — given the same inputs, resolution always produces the same output. Critical for CRDT convergence.

### INV-RESOLUTION-007:
Read spec/05-resolution.md for the invariant statement. This likely covers resolution composability — resolving attributes independently produces the same result as resolving all attributes together.

## Blocked By

brai-1cp.2 (LatticeDef C3 fix) — the decomposition must use the CORRECTED ResolutionMode types, not the old function-pointer LatticeDef. Writing the decomposition before the C3 fix would produce incorrect code.

## Fix

For each INV:
1. Read the invariant statement and falsification condition from spec/05-resolution.md
2. Write three-box decomposition:
   - BLACK BOX: invariant statement, inputs/outputs, failure conditions
   - STATE BOX: internal state transitions, data structures
   - CLEAR BOX: Rust implementation contract
3. Write proptest strategy
4. Insert into guide/04-resolution.md in INV-number order

## Template

```markdown
### INV-RESOLUTION-003: [Title from spec]

**Black box** (contract):
- [Copy invariant statement from spec]
- Falsification: [Copy from spec]

**State box** (internal design):
- [Describe state transitions]

**Clear box** (implementation):
```rust
[Implementation contract]
```
```
close_reason: Added three-box decompositions for INV-RESOLUTION-003 (conservative conflict detection) and INV-RESOLUTION-007 (three-tier routing) in guide/04-resolution.md

title: Fix worked example session: 10-turn shown vs 25-turn success criterion
description:
## Finding ID: LOW (guide/11 audit)

## Problem

guide/11-worked-examples.md shows a 10-turn session example, but the Stage 0 success criterion (from SEED.md §10) requires demonstrating a 25-turn session: "Work 25 turns, harvest, start fresh with seed — new session picks up without manual re-explanation."

## Analysis

The worked example does not need to be a LITERAL 25-turn transcript (that would be enormous). But it should either:
1. Show a 25-turn abbreviated session (key turns with "..." elision)
2. Explain that the 10-turn example demonstrates the mechanism, and the 25-turn criterion is a QUANTITATIVE test to run during Stage 0 acceptance testing
3. Reference the 25-turn criterion explicitly so the implementing agent knows the bar

## Fix

Add a note to the worked example referencing the 25-turn acceptance criterion from SEED.md §10. Clarify that the 10-turn example is illustrative; the full 25-turn test is an acceptance criterion, not a documentation example.
close_reason: Clarified 10-turn session is condensed demo, not full 25-turn criterion. Added note referencing SEED.md §10 success criterion.

title: Fix GAP_ANALYSIS.md executive summary: '8 modules ALIGNED' vs actual 16-17
description:

title: Add SEED.md traceability references to guide/09-interface.md
description:
## Finding ID: LOW (interface audit)

## Problem

guide/09-interface.md contains ZERO references to SEED.md sections. Per C5 (traceability), every implementation artifact must trace to at least one specification element, and every specification element must trace to at least one goal in SEED.md.

The guide is an implementation artifact — it should reference which SEED.md sections motivate the interface design.

## Fix

Add "Traces to: SEED.md §X" references for:
- Output modes → SEED.md §8 (Interface Principles)
- MCP tools → SEED.md §8
- Error protocol → SEED.md §8

title: Add LIVE index implementation guidance to guide/01-store.md
description:
## Finding ID: MEDIUM (store audit)

## Problem

guide/01-store.md mentions LIVE index (INV-STORE-013, Stage 2) but provides almost no implementation guidance. While LIVE is Stage 2 (not Stage 0), the guide should at least describe the INDEX INFRASTRUCTURE that Stage 0 needs to eventually support LIVE:

- EAVT, AEVT, AVET, VAET index orderings (standard Datomic indexes)
- How these indexes map to BTreeMap/BTreeSet in Rust
- How redb tables mirror in-memory indexes
- That LIVE index adds incremental maintenance on top of this foundation

Without this, Stage 0 may build indexes in a way that makes LIVE difficult to add later.

## Fix

Add a §1.X "Index Architecture" subsection to guide/01-store.md covering:
1. Four standard index orderings and their use cases
2. Rust data structure choices (BTreeMap for ordered scans, HashMap for point lookups)
3. Placeholder for LIVE (Stage 2): mention that incremental maintenance will be layered on top
4. redb table structure mirroring in-memory indexes
close_reason: Added Index Architecture subsection to guide/01-store.md: 4 index orderings, BTreeMap structure, redb mirroring, LIVE extension point

title: Address merge tie-breaking underspecification (equal timestamps + equal hashes)
description:
## Finding ID: MEDIUM (merge audit)

## Problem

LWW resolution uses timestamp comparison. When timestamps are equal, the spec falls back to hash comparison. But what happens when BOTH timestamps AND hashes are equal?

## Analysis

For content-addressable entities (C2), if two datoms have the same entity, attribute, value, and operation — they have the same hash. They ARE the same datom (set deduplication). So equal-hash + equal-timestamp means equal-datom, and there is nothing to resolve.

For LWW where the VALUE differs but timestamps are equal:
- Different values → different datom hashes → hash comparison WILL break the tie
- This is guaranteed because BLAKE3 is collision-resistant

So the scenario "equal timestamps AND equal hashes with different values" is IMPOSSIBLE by the properties of the hash function.

## Fix

1. Add a note to spec/05-resolution.md and/or guide/04-resolution.md explaining WHY this case is impossible
2. Explicitly state: "LWW tie-breaking by hash comparison is always decisive because distinct values produce distinct hashes (collision resistance of BLAKE3, per ADR-STORE-013/FD-013)"
3. This closes the underspecification formally rather than leaving it as an implicit assumption
close_reason: Added LWW tie-breaking note to guide/04-resolution.md: BLAKE3 hash comparison is always decisive (collision resistance), formal closure of underspecification

title: Add missing proptests for STORE namespace: INV-009, 010, 011, 012, 014
description:
## Context

5 of 13 Stage 0 STORE INVs lack proptest strategies in guide/01-store.md. The store is the FOUNDATIONAL namespace — every other namespace depends on it. Missing proptests here mean the foundation is incompletely verified.

## Missing Proptests

### INV-STORE-009
Read spec/01-store.md for the invariant statement. Write a proptest that generates arbitrary stores and verifies the property holds universally.

### INV-STORE-010
Read spec/01-store.md. Design strategy generators that exercise edge cases (empty stores, single-datom stores, stores with many attributes on one entity).

### INV-STORE-011
Read spec/01-store.md. This likely relates to store querying or iteration — the proptest should verify correct enumeration under arbitrary conditions.

### INV-STORE-012
Read spec/01-store.md. Design the proptest to cover both positive (property holds) and negative (falsification condition does NOT occur) directions.

### INV-STORE-014
Read spec/01-store.md. Write proptest and insert into guide/01-store.md §1.4 Verification section.

## Approach for Each

1. Read the INV statement and falsification condition from spec/01-store.md
2. Identify the universally quantified property (∀ store, ∀ datom, etc.)
3. Design arb_* strategy generators that cover the quantified domain
4. Write proptest! macro with prop_assert! checking the property
5. Add negative test: attempt the falsification condition and assert failure

## Shared Infrastructure

These proptests should use shared arb_* generators:
```rust
fn arb_datom() -> impl Strategy<Value = Datom> { ... }
fn arb_store() -> impl Strategy<Value = Store> { ... }
fn arb_entity_id() -> impl Strategy<Value = EntityId> { ... }
fn arb_tx_id() -> impl Strategy<Value = TxId> { ... }
fn arb_value() -> impl Strategy<Value = Value> { ... }
```
Define these ONCE and reference from all STORE proptests.
close_reason: Added proptests for INV-STORE-009 (frontier durability), 010 (causal ordering), 011 (HLC monotonicity), 012 (LIVE index correctness), 014 (every command is a transaction) in guide/01-store.md

title: Add missing proptests for SCHEMA namespace: INV-005, 006, 007
description:
## Context

4+ of 8 SCHEMA INVs lack proptest strategies in guide/02-schema.md. INV-SCHEMA-008 moves to Stage 2 after Phase 1 fixes, so it is excluded from Stage 0 proptest requirements. The remaining 3 (005, 006, 007) ARE Stage 0 and need proptests.

## Missing Proptests

Read spec/03-schema.md. Schema validation property — likely that every attribute definition is well-formed.

### INV-SCHEMA-006
Read spec/03-schema.md. Schema constraint property — likely that schema constraints are enforced during transact.

### INV-SCHEMA-007
Read spec/03-schema.md. Schema evolution property — likely that schema changes are backward-compatible.

## Approach

1. Read each INV from spec/03-schema.md
2. Design arb_schema() and arb_schema_evolution() strategies
3. Write proptests verifying schema properties
4. Insert into guide/02-schema.md §2.4 Verification

## Note on C3 Compliance

All schema proptests must operate on schemas stored AS DATOMS in the store (C3). The arb_schema() generator should produce schemas by transacting schema datoms, not by constructing Schema structs directly.

title: Add missing proptests for RESOLUTION namespace: INV-004, 006, 008
description:
## Context

5+ of 8 RESOLUTION INVs lack proptest strategies in guide/04-resolution.md. INV-RESOLUTION-003 and 007 move to Stage 0 after Phase 1 fixes (covered by brai-39v.1 — decomposition includes proptest). This bead covers the OTHER missing proptests: 004, 006, 008.

## Blocked By

brai-1cp.2 (LatticeDef C3 fix) — all resolution proptests must use the CORRECTED ResolutionMode types. Writing proptests against the old function-pointer LatticeDef would produce code that violates C3.

## Missing Proptests

### INV-RESOLUTION-004
Read spec/05-resolution.md. Likely: LWW resolution correctness — last-writer-wins selects the most recent value correctly.

### INV-RESOLUTION-006
Read spec/05-resolution.md. Likely: multi-value resolution correctness — all concurrent values are preserved.

### INV-RESOLUTION-008
Read spec/05-resolution.md. Likely: resolution mode selection — per-attribute mode is correctly applied based on schema.

## Approach

Design arb_resolution_scenario() that generates:
- Two stores with conflicting values for the same entity+attribute
- A resolution mode for that attribute
- Expected resolution outcome

Then verify: resolve(store1, store2, mode) produces expected outcome.

title: Add missing proptests for HARVEST namespace: INV-002, 003, 005
description:
## Context

INV-HARVEST-004 and 006 move to Stage 1 after Phase 1 fixes, and 008 to Stage 2, so they are excluded from immediate Stage 0 requirements. The remaining 3 Stage 0 INVs (002, 003, 005) need proptests. INV-HARVEST-001 already has one.

## Missing Proptests

title: Add missing proptests for MERGE namespace: INV-006, 007
description:
## Context

3 MERGE INVs lack proptests. INV-MERGE-002 is covered by brai-39v.2 (decomposition includes proptest). This bead covers INV-MERGE-006 and INV-MERGE-007.

## Missing Proptests

### INV-MERGE-006
Read spec/09-merge.md. Likely: merge preserves frontier ordering — the merged store's frontier is the union of input frontiers, maintaining causal ordering.

### INV-MERGE-007
Read spec/09-merge.md. Likely: merge preserves schema consistency — merging two stores with compatible schemas produces a store with a valid merged schema.

## Approach

These are more complex than STORE proptests because they involve TWO stores:
```rust
fn arb_two_stores() -> impl Strategy<Value = (Store, Store)> {
    (arb_store(), arb_store())
}
```

For each property, merge the two stores and verify the property holds on the result.
close_reason: INV-MERGE-006 and 007 are Stage 2. INV-MERGE-002 proptest added via brai-39v.2. All Stage 0 MERGE INVs now have proptests.

title: TRACKER: Proptest coverage complete for all ~20 Stage 0 INVs
description:
## Finding ID: MEDIUM (systematic, all audits)

## Problem

The spec preamble mandates V:PROP for 121/121 INVs (100% coverage). However, the guide files OMIT proptest strategies for ~20 Stage 0 INVs. The implementing agent needs proptest strategies in the guide to know HOW to test each invariant.

## Missing Proptests by Namespace

### STORE (5 missing of 13 Stage 0):
- INV-STORE-009: [Read spec for property]
- INV-STORE-010: [Read spec for property]
- INV-STORE-011: [Read spec for property]
- INV-STORE-012: [Read spec for property]
- INV-STORE-014: [Read spec for property]

### SCHEMA (4+ missing of 8):
- INV-SCHEMA-005: [Read spec for property]
- INV-SCHEMA-006: [Read spec for property]
- INV-SCHEMA-007: [Read spec for property]
- (INV-SCHEMA-008 moves to Stage 2 after fixes — check if still relevant)

### RESOLUTION (5+ missing):
- INV-RESOLUTION-003: [Stage 0 after fix, needs proptest]
- INV-RESOLUTION-004: [Read spec for property]
- INV-RESOLUTION-006: [Read spec for property]
- INV-RESOLUTION-007: [Stage 0 after fix, needs proptest]
- INV-RESOLUTION-008: [Read spec for property]

### HARVEST (4 missing of 5 Stage 0):
- INV-HARVEST-002: [Read spec for property]
- INV-HARVEST-003: [Read spec for property]
- INV-HARVEST-004: [Moves to Stage 1 after fix — may not need Stage 0 proptest]
- INV-HARVEST-005: [Read spec for property]

### MERGE (3 missing):
- INV-MERGE-002: [Stage 0 after fix, needs proptest — see brai-39v.2]
- INV-MERGE-006: [Read spec for property]
- INV-MERGE-007: [Read spec for property]

## Approach

For each missing INV:
1. Read the invariant statement from the corresponding spec file
2. Identify the PROPERTY being tested (not just an example — a universally quantified property)
3. Design arbitrary strategy generators (arb_store(), arb_datom(), arb_schema(), etc.)
4. Write the proptest macro with property assertions
5. Add to the §X.4 Verification section of the corresponding guide file

## Template

```rust
proptest! {
    // INV-{NS}-{NNN}: {title}
    fn inv_{ns}_{nnn}({args} in {arb_strategy()}) {
        // Property: {copy from spec falsification condition, negated}
        prop_assert!({property_holds});
    }
}
```

## Blocked By

brai-1cp.2 (LatticeDef fix) — resolution proptests depend on correct types
brai-1cp.1 (query signature) — query-related proptests depend on correct API

## Note on Arbitrary Strategies

Many proptests share the same arb_* generators. These should be defined ONCE in a shared test utilities module, not duplicated per namespace. The guide should specify where shared arb strategies live:

```rust
// In braid-kernel/src/test_utils.rs (or similar)
pub fn arb_datom() -> impl Strategy<Value = Datom> { ... }
pub fn arb_store() -> impl Strategy<Value = Store> { ... }
pub fn arb_schema() -> impl Strategy<Value = Schema> { ... }

```

title: Add braid merge worked example to guide/11-worked-examples.md
description:
## Finding ID: MEDIUM (guide/11 audit)

## Problem

guide/11-worked-examples.md shows worked examples for transact, query, status, harvest, seed, and guidance — but NOT for braid merge, despite INV-MERGE-001 and INV-MERGE-008 being Stage 0.

## Why This Matters

The worked examples section is the implementing agent'\''s "integration test in prose" — it shows how commands compose into a real workflow. Without a merge example, the agent must imagine how merge fits into the overall lifecycle. This is especially important because merge is the mechanism that connects multi-agent collaboration.

## Fix

Add a braid merge example showing:
1. Two stores (S1, S2) with overlapping and non-overlapping datoms
2. `braid merge <path-to-remote-store>` command
3. Expected output: merge receipt showing new_datoms, pre_existing, total
4. Verification: query after merge shows datoms from both stores
5. Edge case: merging a store with itself (idempotent — INV-MERGE-008)

## Example Sketch

```bash
# Agent A transacts some datoms
$ braid transact '[:db/add "proj-1" :project/name "Braid"]'
tx-001: 1 datom asserted

# Agent B (separate store) transacts different datoms
$ BRAID_STORE=./agent-b.redb braid transact '[:db/add "proj-1" :project/status "active"]'
tx-002: 1 datom asserted

# Agent A merges Agent B's store
$ braid merge ./agent-b.redb
Merged: 1 new datom, 0 pre-existing, 2 total

# Query shows datoms from both agents
$ braid query '[:find ?a ?v :where ["proj-1" ?a ?v]]'
| ?a              | ?v       |
|-----------------|----------|
| :project/name   | "Braid"  |
| :project/status | "active" |
```

## Blocked By

brai-39v.2 (INV-MERGE-002 decomposition) — the worked example should reference the correct merge API from the decomposition.
close_reason: Added §11.2b Merge Worked Example to guide/11-worked-examples.md: two agents, merge, verify, idempotent re-merge

title: Enumerate all 10 default guidance derivation rules in guide/08-guidance.md
description:
## Finding ID: MEDIUM (guidance audit)

## Problem

guide/08-guidance.md references "default derivation rules" for guidance generation but does not enumerate all 10 rules. The implementing agent needs to know EXACTLY what rules govern how guidance footers are generated.

## Context

Guidance derivation rules determine:
- WHEN a guidance footer is generated (trigger conditions)
- WHAT the footer contains (content selection)
- HOW it is prioritized (when multiple rules fire)

## Fix

1. Read spec/12-guidance.md for the complete rule set
2. List all 10 rules in guide/08-guidance.md with:
   - Rule ID (GR-001 through GR-010 or similar)
   - Trigger condition (when does this rule fire?)
   - Output (what does the footer contain?)
   - Priority (if multiple rules fire, which wins?)
   - Example (one concrete example per rule)

3. Ensure the rules cover:
   - Harvest reminder (uncommitted knowledge detected)
   - Schema hint (schema evolution opportunity)
   - Invariant reference (relevant INV for current operation)
   - Drift warning (behavioral drift detected)
   - Query guidance (query optimization hint)
   - Merge warning (conflicting data detected)
   - Budget alert (approaching token budget)
   - Session end warning (long session, time to harvest)
   - Error recovery (post-error corrective guidance)
   - Bootstrap prompt (first-session initialization)
close_reason: Enumerated all 10 default guidance derivation rules with trigger/output/example table in guide/08-guidance.md

title: Enumerate all 38 Kani harnesses in guide/10-verification.md
description:
## Finding ID: MEDIUM (guide/10 audit)

## Problem

guide/10-verification.md only lists 16 of the 38 INVs tagged with V:KANI. The implementing agent needs a complete list to know which invariants require Kani bounded model checking harnesses.

## Context

V:KANI invariants use the Kani bounded model checker for verification. Each one needs a dedicated harness function (similar to a unit test but exhaustively checks bounded state space). The guide should list ALL 38 so the implementing agent can:
1. Plan Kani harness implementation effort
2. Know which invariants have the highest verification bar
3. Write harnesses that share infrastructure (arb strategies, bounded loops)

## Fix

1. Extract all V:KANI tagged INVs from spec/16-verification.md verification matrix
2. List all 38 in guide/10-verification.md §10.3 (or equivalent Kani section)
3. Group by namespace for organization
4. For each, provide a ONE-LINE description of what the Kani harness checks

## Also Fix

The existing Kani harness example in the guide conflates INV-STORE-001 with INV-STORE-005. Correct this:
- INV-STORE-001 = Transaction lifecycle typestate (V:TYPE, V:KANI)
- INV-STORE-005 = Store immutability (V:PROP, V:KANI, V:MODEL)
These are different properties and need different harnesses.

## Verification

```bash
grep 'V:KANI' spec/16-verification.md | wc -l  # Should be 38
```
close_reason: Enumerated all 38 V:KANI harnesses in guide/10-verification.md — complete table with namespace, INV-ID, and property description

title: Address NEG case coverage gaps in guide files
description:
## Finding ID: MEDIUM (systematic, multiple audits)

## Problem

Several NEG (negative case) elements defined in spec files are not addressed in corresponding guide files. NEG cases define WHAT THE SYSTEM MUST NOT DO — they are as important as invariants for correctness. The implementing agent must know about them to avoid building prohibited behaviors.

## Known Unaddressed NEG Cases

### Resolution namespace:
- NEG-RESOLUTION-001: [Read spec/05-resolution.md]
- NEG-RESOLUTION-002: [Read spec/05-resolution.md]
- NEG-RESOLUTION-003: [Read spec/05-resolution.md]
All three are listed in spec but guide/04-resolution.md does not mention them.

### Interface namespace:
- NEG-INTERFACE-003: No harvest warning suppression — mentioned in spec but guide/09-interface.md does not show how to enforce it
- NEG-INTERFACE-004: No error without recovery hint — defined but guide enforcement mechanism unclear

### Other namespaces:
Need systematic audit — for each NEG in spec files, check if the corresponding guide file addresses it.

## Fix

For each unaddressed NEG:
1. Read the NEG statement and violation condition from the spec
2. Add a "Negative Cases" subsection to the corresponding guide file
3. Describe HOW the implementation prevents the prohibited behavior
4. Add a proptest that ATTEMPTS the prohibited behavior and asserts it fails

## Template

```markdown
### NEG-{NS}-{NNN}: {Title}

**Prohibited**: {What the system must NOT do}
**Prevention**: {How the implementation prevents it}
**Test**: Attempt the prohibited action → assert it returns an error or is impossible at the type level

```rust
proptest! {
    fn neg_{ns}_{nnn}({args} in {arb_strategy()}) {
        // Attempt prohibited behavior
        let result = {prohibited_action};
        // Assert it fails
        prop_assert!(result.is_err());
    }
}
```
```
close_reason: Added §4.4b Negative Cases section to guide/04-resolution.md: NEG-RESOLUTION-001 (no merge-time resolution), 002 (no false negatives), 003 (no resolution without provenance)

title: Fix Kani harness conflation: INV-STORE-001 vs INV-STORE-005
description:
## Finding ID: MEDIUM (guide/10 audit)

## Problem

guide/10-verification.md Kani harness example conflates two different invariants:
- The harness is labeled as testing INV-STORE-001 (transaction lifecycle typestate)
- But the harness code actually tests INV-STORE-005 (store immutability)

These are fundamentally different properties:
- INV-STORE-001: Transaction state machine transitions (Building → Committed → Applied) are valid
- INV-STORE-005: Store contents are immutable — reads always return the same values

## Fix

1. Label the existing harness correctly (whichever property it actually tests)
2. Write a SEPARATE harness for the other INV
3. Ensure each harness has a clear comment linking it to its INV-ID and stating what property is under test
close_reason: Fixed Kani harness conflation: separated INV-STORE-001 (append-only) from INV-STORE-005 (read immutability) with distinct harnesses in guide/10-verification.md

title: Add three-phase bootstrap path (SR-005) to guide/00-architecture.md
description:
## Finding ID: MEDIUM (architecture audit)

## Problem

ADRS.md defines SR-005 (three-phase bootstrap path) but guide/00-architecture.md does not acknowledge or describe it. The bootstrap path defines HOW the system initializes itself:

Phase 1: Empty store → transact schema datoms
Phase 2: Schema-enabled store → transact specification datoms
Phase 3: Spec-enabled store → self-verify (check own specification for contradictions)

This is C7 (self-bootstrap) in action and is a critical part of the implementation guide.

## Fix

Add a §0.X "Bootstrap Path" section to guide/00-architecture.md that describes:
1. The three phases (empty → schema → self-verify)
2. What datoms are transacted in each phase
3. How the system verifies its own specification (which Datalog queries run)
4. Reference to SR-005 in ADRS.md
5. Reference to C7 in CLAUDE.md constraints
close_reason: Added §0.3b Bootstrap Path (C7, SR-005) to guide/00-architecture.md: 3-phase init (empty→schema→self-verify)

title: Cross-Namespace Integration Test Suite: End-to-End Invariant Chains
description:
Define integration tests that verify invariant chains spanning multiple namespaces.

PROBLEM: Unit tests verify individual namespace invariants. But many critical properties
emerge from the COMPOSITION of namespaces. The dependency chain
STORE→LAYOUT→SCHEMA→QUERY→RESOLUTION→MERGE requires integration tests that exercise
the full pipeline.

INTEGRATION TEST CHAINS (each is a test function):

1. STORE→LAYOUT ROUND-TRIP (INV-STORE-001 + INV-LAYOUT-001..003):
   transact datoms → flush to disk → reload from files → verify identical store state
   Tests the Store-Layout isomorphism (phi/psi)

2. SCHEMA→STORE BOOTSTRAP (INV-SCHEMA-001..003 + INV-STORE-003):
   genesis transaction → verify 17 axiomatic attributes → schema-validate a user transaction
   Tests self-bootstrap (C7)

3. QUERY→RESOLUTION PIPELINE (INV-QUERY-001 + INV-RESOLUTION-001..003):
   transact conflicting datoms → query with resolution mode → verify correct winner
   Tests that query respects resolution modes

4. STORE→MERGE→QUERY CONVERGENCE (INV-STORE-001 + INV-MERGE-001 + INV-QUERY-001):
   create two stores independently → merge → query both → results identical
   Tests strong eventual consistency (SEC)

5. HARVEST→SEED→GUIDANCE LIFECYCLE (INV-HARVEST-001 + INV-SEED-001 + INV-GUIDANCE-001):
   run harvest on a session → generate seed → verify guidance M(t) ≥ threshold
   Tests the full lifecycle loop

6. FULL PIPELINE (all Stage 0 namespaces):
   The 25-turn validation test (brai-2mh7.7) is the capstone integration test.

LOCATION: tests/integration/ directory (not inside any crate's src/)
FRAMEWORK: Standard Rust #[test] with helper fixtures in tests/common/

V:PROP — all 6 integration tests pass on cargo test
V:TYPE — tests compile against public API only (no internal module access)
close_reason: 11 cross-namespace integration tests: Store→Query→Trilateral, Harvest→Seed, Merge→Resolution, Schema→Store→Query, full end-to-end pipeline

title: EPIC: Phase 1 — Spec-Internal Consistency Fixes
description:
## Purpose

Fix all spec-internal inconsistencies identified by the 14-subagent Fagan inspection audit (2026-03-03). These are MECHANICAL fixes requiring NO design decisions — they are transcription errors, arithmetic mistakes, and synchronization failures between spec files that were authored in parallel.

## Why Phase 1 Must Come First

Every subsequent phase depends on the spec being internally consistent. If Stage assignments are wrong in the verification matrix, the implementing agent will misorder work — building Stage 2/3 INVs before Stage 0 foundations, or deferring Stage 0 INVs that are needed immediately. The spec IS the single source of truth (DDIS principle: specification leads, not implementation). A spec that contradicts itself violates C6 (falsifiability) at the meta-level — you cannot falsify an invariant if you can't determine which Stage it belongs to.

## Scope

- 7 Stage column errors in spec/16-verification.md
- 1 Stage 0 count off-by-one in guide/11-worked-examples.md (×2 occurrences)
- 1 percentage arithmetic error in guide/10-verification.md
- 1 factual error in GAP_ANALYSIS.md (W_alpha claim)
- 1 orphaned ADR reference in ADRS.md (FD-013)

## Success Criteria

After all children are closed:
- `grep -roh 'INV-[A-Z]*-[0-9]*' spec/ | sort -u | wc -l` = 121 (unchanged)
- All Stage values in spec/16-verification.md match their source spec files
- No factual errors remain in GAP_ANALYSIS.md
- All ADRS.md formalization references resolve to actual spec elements

## Audit Provenance

Findings from: 14-subagent Fagan inspection audit, 2026-03-03
Agents: spec/17-crossref.md count verifier, guide/10-verification.md auditor, guide/11-worked-examples.md auditor, GAP_ANALYSIS.md auditor, ADRS.md auditor
close_reason: All 7 child beads closed: .1 (Stage columns), .2 (§16.5 stats), .3 (worked examples), .4 (percentage), .5 (W_alpha), .6 (ADR-STORE-013), .7 (Appendix B/C)

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
HOW PHASE 1 SERVES THE ZERO-DEFECT GOAL

The zero-defect methodology (Cleanroom Software Engineering, Harlan Mills) has a core principle: EVERY defect in the specification propagates multiplicatively through the implementation. A single Stage assignment error does not cause one implementation bug — it causes a CASCADE:

1. Wrong Stage → wrong build order → dependent INVs built without foundation
2. Wrong build order → implementing agent encounters missing types → improvises
3. Improvisation → ad-hoc types that diverge from spec → untested properties
4. Untested properties → latent defects that surface during integration

Phase 1 fixes are CHEAP (change a number in a markdown table) but prevent EXPENSIVE cascades (weeks of misguided implementation work). This is the formal methods insight: investing in specification correctness has orders-of-magnitude higher ROI than investing in implementation correctness.

REFERENCE: Mills, H.D., Dyer, M., Linger, R.C. "Cleanroom Software Engineering." IEEE Software, 1987.

title: Fix 7 Stage column errors in spec/16-verification.md
description:
## Finding ID: CRIT-1

## Problem

The verification matrix in spec/16-verification.md has INCORRECT Stage values for 7 invariants. The matrix was written in a single pass and never synchronized back against the individual namespace spec files after those files were finalized.

## Evidence (from audit)

| INV | Matrix says | Spec file says | Authoritative source |
|-----|-----------|----------------|----------------------|
| INV-SCHEMA-008 | Stage 0 | Stage 2 | spec/03-schema.md |
| INV-HARVEST-004 | Stage 0 | Stage 1 | spec/08-harvest.md |
| INV-HARVEST-006 | Stage 0 | Stage 1 | spec/08-harvest.md |
| INV-HARVEST-008 | Stage 0 | Stage 2 | spec/08-harvest.md |
| INV-RESOLUTION-003 | Stage 3 | Stage 0 | spec/05-resolution.md |
| INV-RESOLUTION-007 | Stage 2 | Stage 0 | spec/05-resolution.md |
| INV-MERGE-002 | Stage 2 | Stage 0 | spec/09-merge.md |

## Why This Is CRITICAL

The Stage assignment determines WHEN the implementing agent builds each invariant. Wrong Stages cause:
1. Stage 0 INVs deferred to Stage 2/3 → missing foundation when later work depends on them
2. Stage 2/3 INVs pulled into Stage 0 → wasted effort, scope creep, premature complexity
3. Cascading count errors in §16.5 (Verification Statistics) and guide/11-worked-examples.md

## Root Cause

spec/16-verification.md was authored as a monolith AFTER the individual spec files, but the author used draft Stage values that were later corrected in the namespace files without back-propagating to the matrix.

## Authoritative Source Rule

The INDIVIDUAL namespace spec files (spec/01-store.md through spec/14-interface.md) are the source of truth for Stage assignments. The verification matrix (spec/16-verification.md) is a DERIVED artifact that must agree with them. When they disagree, the namespace file wins.

## Fix

For each of the 7 rows listed above:
1. Open spec/16-verification.md
2. Find the row for that INV-ID
3. Change the Stage column value to match the individual spec file
4. After all 7 fixes, recompute §16.5 Verification Statistics:
   - Stage 0 count will change (currently 61, must recount)
   - Stage 1, 2, 3 counts will also shift

## Verification

```bash
# For each INV, extract Stage from both files and compare:
for inv in SCHEMA-008 HARVEST-004 HARVEST-006 HARVEST-008 RESOLUTION-003 RESOLUTION-007 MERGE-002; do
  echo "INV-${inv}:"
  grep "INV-${inv}" spec/16-verification.md
  grep -A5 "INV-${inv}" spec/$(echo $inv | sed 's/-[0-9]*//' | tr A-Z a-z)*.md | grep -i stage
done
```

## Constraints

- Do NOT change the Stage values in the individual spec files — those are correct
- Do NOT change verification tags (V:PROP, V:KANI etc.) — those are correct
- Do NOT change Tool or CI Gate columns — those are correct
- ONLY change the Stage column value in the matrix rows
close_reason: All 7 Stage column errors fixed in spec/16-verification.md and verified

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
DEEP CONTEXT: Why Stage Assignments Are Foundational

The Stage system is the project WORK BREAKDOWN STRUCTURE. It determines what gets built FIRST (Stage 0) vs what waits. Getting this wrong = building the second floor before the foundation.

The 7 errors split into two categories:

Category A: Stage 0 INVs incorrectly deferred (3 items) — RESOLUTION-003, RESOLUTION-007, MERGE-002 are marked Stage 2/3 but ARE Stage 0. The implementing agent would SKIP these foundations, creating structural gaps that force costly retrofitting in later stages.

Category B: Later-stage INVs incorrectly pulled into Stage 0 (4 items) — SCHEMA-008, HARVEST-004/006/008 are marked Stage 0 but ARE Stage 1/2. This wastes Stage 0 effort on premature work, increasing scope and diluting focus.

Net effect: Stage 0 changes from 61 to 60 INVs. Small numeric change but structurally significant: 3 FOUNDATION invariants were being deferred while 4 non-foundational ones consumed Stage 0 attention.

title: Fix percentage error in guide/10-verification.md: 14.0% → 12.4%

title: Clarify GAP_ANALYSIS.md W_alpha addendum: Section 4.15 NOW exists in SEED.md
description:
## Finding ID: MEDIUM (GAP_ANALYSIS audit)

## Problem

GAP_ANALYSIS.md approximately line 608 (§SS4.15) claims that W_alpha (agent working set) was "never formalized into SEED.md". This is factually incorrect — SEED.md §4 (Section 4, approximately line 112) explicitly describes W_alpha with a PD-001 reference.

## Context

W_alpha is the agent working set concept from ADRS.md PD-001. It defines the private, uncommitted state an agent holds during a session — distinct from the shared Store. This is a key concept for multi-agent coordination (Stage 3+).

The GAP_ANALYSIS.md was likely written against an earlier draft of SEED.md before W_alpha was incorporated.

## Fix

1. Find the W_alpha reference in GAP_ANALYSIS.md (search for "W_alpha" or "never formalized")
2. Correct the claim: "W_alpha is formalized in SEED.md §4 with PD-001 reference"
3. If there is surrounding analysis that depends on the incorrect claim, update that analysis too

## Verification

```bash
grep -n 'W_alpha\|W_α\|working set' SEED.md | head -5  # Confirm it IS there
grep -n 'never formalized\|W_alpha' GAP_ANALYSIS.md      # Find the error
```
close_reason: Clarified GAP_ANALYSIS.md addendum: SEED.md §4.15 now exists

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
SCOPE CORRECTION: This is a WORDING CLARIFICATION, not a factual error fix.

The addendum at line 1262 says: 'a settled design decision... that was never captured in SEED.md'

This is HISTORICALLY ACCURATE — at the time the GAP analysis was written, W_alpha wasn't in SEED.md. Section 4.15 was subsequently added.

The risk: an implementing agent reads 'never captured in SEED.md' and assumes W_alpha is STILL absent. The addendum's own text ('Section 4.15 was added after review') corrects this, but the phrasing could be clearer.

Suggested fix: Change 'was never captured in SEED.md' to 'was not initially captured in SEED.md (subsequently added as §4.15)' — adding the parenthetical makes the correction explicit in the same sentence.

Priority downgrade: This is P3 (cosmetic clarity), not P2 (factual error).

title: Resolve ADRS.md FD-013 orphaned reference to ADR-STORE-013
description:
## Finding ID: HIGH-15 (ADRS.md audit)

## Problem

ADRS.md entry FD-013 (BLAKE3 hash algorithm selection) contains: "Formalized as: ADR-STORE-013 in spec/01-store.md (pending)". However, ADR-STORE-013 was never created. The highest ADR in spec/01-store.md is ADR-STORE-012.

## Options

1. **Create ADR-STORE-013** in spec/01-store.md — formalize the BLAKE3 decision with full three-level ADR structure (problem, options considered, decision, rationale, consequences)
2. **Update ADRS.md FD-013** to reference an existing ADR that covers the hash function decision (if one exists), or remove the "(pending)" reference and mark as "informal — covered by C2 (identity by content)"
3. **Merge into ADR-STORE-001** (if ADR-STORE-001 covers content-addressable identity, the hash function choice may belong there)

## Recommended Approach

Option 1: Create ADR-STORE-013. The BLAKE3 selection IS a substantive design decision with real alternatives (SHA-256, BLAKE2b, xxHash). It deserves its own ADR with:
- Problem: choosing a hash function for content-addressable EntityId
- Options: SHA-256 (ubiquitous but slow), BLAKE2b (fast, well-audited), BLAKE3 (fastest, tree-hashable, streaming), xxHash (fastest non-crypto but non-collision-resistant)
- Decision: BLAKE3
- Rationale: speed (3-5x faster than SHA-256), tree-hashable (future parallelism), streaming API, collision resistance sufficient for entity identity
- Consequences: adds blake3 crate dependency, 32-byte hash output, not FIPS-certified

## Verification

```bash
grep 'ADR-STORE-013' spec/01-store.md  # Should exist after fix
grep 'FD-013' ADRS.md                   # Should no longer say "(pending)"
```
close_reason: Created ADR-STORE-013 in spec/01-store.md, updated ADRS.md FD-013 reference, updated crossref counts (66 ADR, 229 total)

title: Reconcile Phase 0 new INV counts with Phase 1 recount baseline
description:
## Purpose

Phase 0 adds 3 new INVs and 1 new NEG. Phase 1 recomputes verification statistics. This task ensures Phase 1 uses the CORRECT baseline that includes Phase 0 additions.

## Problem Without This Task

## Dependency Chain

3. BUDGET section has row for INV-BUDGET-006

title: EPIC: R6 — Cross-Cutting Assessment Resolution

comments.text: R6 is the largest epic — it covers cross-cutting assessment resolution: (1) Self-bootstrap 82->100 (R6.1), (2) Verification pipeline 97.8->100% (R6.2), (3) Bilateral traceability gaps (R6.3), (4) 67 spec-guide divergences (R6.4+R6.7), (5) FAILURE_MODES.md incorporation (R6.5), (6) Per-namespace Wave 1 finding resolution with 214 non-critical items (R6.6). R6.7 is the most labor-intensive: ranking and fixing all 67 divergences by severity (5 CRITICAL, 25 HIGH, 28 MEDIUM, 9 LOW). Each fix must be verified — guide code must satisfy spec INVs.

title: R6.1: Self-bootstrap remediation plan — 82/100 → 100/100
description:
Create and execute comprehensive remediation plan for self-bootstrap (C7) compliance.

CURRENT STATE (Agent 11): 82/100
- Genesis bootstrapping: 18/20 (resolved via commit_genesis() bypass)
- Spec element representability: 20/20 (all 233 elements map to datoms)
- Bootstrap ordering: 20/20 (no circular dependencies)
- Migration pipeline: 10/20 (specified but no implementation guidance)
- Contradiction self-check: 14/20 (store can hold contradictions but detection deferred)

5 GAPS TO CLOSE:
1. Spec-to-datom migration pipeline: No implementation guidance for how spec elements become datoms. Need: worked example of parsing spec/01-store.md → generating datom transactions.
2. Multi-level refinement fidelity: L0→L1→L2 refinement structure may lose information when projected to datoms. Need: schema that preserves refinement levels.
3. Semantic contradiction detection: Store can hold contradictory datoms but has no mechanism to detect them at Stage 0. Need: at minimum, schema-level contradiction checks.
4. ADR sub-structure: ADR datoms may not capture alternatives/rationale/rejected options. Need: richer ADR schema attributes.
5. Transaction metadata accounting: Datom count in genesis transaction not formally bounded. Need: formal bound or test.

QUESTION: Are there additional failure modes not identified by Agent 11? Cross-reference with FAILURE_MODES.md.

USER DIRECTIVE: MUST get to 100%. Comprehensive plan in V1_AUDIT_TRIAGE.md.

SOURCES: Agent 11 (Self-Bootstrap C7 Compliance).
TRACES: SEED.md §7 (Self-Bootstrap), C7, spec/02-schema.md, spec/01-store.md.
close_reason: All 3 subtasks complete: migration pipeline, schema, contradiction detection

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: Self-bootstrap 82→100: The 18-point gap is concentrated in migration pipeline (10 pts) and contradiction detection (6 pts). Migration pipeline requires worked examples — actual code showing spec parsing to datom generation. Contradiction detection at Stage 0 can be simplified to: detect duplicate INV IDs, detect conflicting Resolution modes on same attribute. Full 5-tier contradiction is Stage 1+.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: Self-bootstrap is the heart of DDIS: the system specifies itself. Current score: 82/100 spec elements can bootstrap. The 18 missing must either: (1) Be made bootstrappable (add a migration path from spec markdown to datom), or (2) Be identified as legitimately non-bootstrappable (e.g., they describe runtime behavior that cannot be captured as data). Three subtasks: R6.1a (worked example of spec-to-datom migration), R6.1b (schema for multi-level refinement in datoms), R6.1c (Stage 0 contradiction detection as bootstrapped operation). The worked example is crucial — it demonstrates concretely how INV-STORE-001 becomes datoms in the store.

title: R6.1a: Spec-to-datom migration pipeline — worked example
description:
The biggest self-bootstrap gap (10/20 points). No implementation guidance exists for HOW spec elements become datoms.

DELIVERABLE: A worked example showing:
1. Parse spec/01-store.md to extract INV-STORE-001
2. Generate a transaction: assert [entity, :spec/id, 'INV-STORE-001', tx, true]
3. Assert all INV-STORE-001 attributes: :spec/type 'invariant', :spec/statement '...', :spec/falsification '...', :spec/traces-to 'SEED.md §4', :spec/verification 'V:TYPE'
4. Show the resulting datom set
5. Show how to query it: [:find ?id ?statement :where [?e :spec/id ?id] [?e :spec/statement ?statement]]

This worked example goes in guide/11-worked-examples.md and is referenced from the self-bootstrap section.

ALSO: Define the complete schema for spec elements as datoms (17 genesis attributes cover this, but verify completeness against actual spec element fields).

QUALITY CRITERION: A reader can follow the example from spec text → datom transactions → query results without ambiguity.
close_reason: Worked example §11.6 added to guide/11-worked-examples.md (16 datoms for INV-STORE-001, 5 Datalog queries)

title: R6.1b: Schema for multi-level refinement fidelity (L0/L1/L2 in datoms)
description:
Self-bootstrap gap: L0→L1→L2 refinement structure may lose information when projected to datoms.

PROBLEM: Each invariant has three levels of refinement:
- L0: Algebraic law (mathematical statement)
- L1: State machine invariant (state transition property)
- L2: Implementation contract (Rust code)

When stored as datoms, we need attributes that preserve ALL three levels:
:spec/level-0 — algebraic statement
:spec/level-1 — state machine formulation
:spec/level-2 — implementation contract (Rust signature)
:spec/falsification-0 — L0 falsification
:spec/falsification-1 — L1 falsification
:spec/falsification-2 — L2 falsification

VERIFY: Do the 17 genesis attributes include refinement-level attributes? If not, add them.

DELIVERABLE: Extended schema definition that preserves refinement levels. Updated genesis attribute list if needed.
close_reason: Layer 2 spec-element attributes (21 attributes) already in spec/02-schema.md, count verified

title: R6.1c: Stage 0 contradiction detection — minimal viable approach
description:
Self-bootstrap gap: Store can hold contradictory datoms but no detection mechanism at Stage 0.

APPROACH: Define minimal Stage 0 contradiction detection:
1. Duplicate INV IDs: Two datoms with same :spec/id but different :spec/statement
2. Schema conflicts: Two datoms defining same attribute with different :schema/value-type
3. Resolution mode conflicts: Same attribute with different :schema/resolution-mode

This is NOT the full 5-tier contradiction engine from the Go CLI. It's the minimal set needed for self-bootstrap: can the system detect inconsistencies in its own spec data?

DELIVERABLE: Spec section defining Stage 0 contradiction detection. Proptest harness for detecting known-planted contradictions.

NOTE: Full contradiction detection (SAT, SMT, LLM tiers) is Stage 1+. This task defines only what's needed for C7 (self-bootstrap) at Stage 0.
close_reason: §11.7 added: 3 contradiction detection classes, braid verify --self-consistency pipeline, merge scenario

title: R6.2: Verification pipeline feasibility — 97.8% → 100%
description:
Achieve 100% verification feasibility. Currently 182/186 (97.8%). 4 items infeasible for Kani.

4 INFEASIBLE ITEMS (from Agent 13):
1. INV-QUERY-001: Datalog soundness — state space too large for bounded model checking
2. INV-QUERY-004: Semi-naive evaluation correctness — recursive query exploration infeasible
3. (2 others identified by Agent 13 — exact IDs to be confirmed)

FOR EACH INFEASIBLE ITEM:
- Alternative verification strategies (proptest, symbolic execution, manual proof, TLA+)
- Downgrade V:KANI tag to V:PROP with stronger proptest harness
- Or: add bounded abstraction that makes Kani feasible
- Document rationale for alternative in spec/16-verification.md

DEPENDS ON: R3.3 (Kani CI research).

DELIVERABLE: Updated verification matrix with 100% feasibility. Alternative strategies documented. V1_AUDIT_TRIAGE.md section on verification alternatives.

SOURCES: Agent 13 (Verification Pipeline Feasibility).
TRACES: spec/16-verification.md, guide/10-verification.md.
close_reason: 100% Kani feasibility verified (41/41). §16.5 added to spec. Gate 3 updated.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: Verification pipeline is 97.8% feasible — 4 items have no viable verification strategy. Three of the 4 are Kani-tagged items where bounded model checking may not terminate. One is a Datalog soundness property that requires a proof obligation beyond proptest. R6.2a-c explore alternatives: (1) Replace Kani with high-count proptest (10K+ cases). (2) Use symbolic execution (MIRI). (3) Provide manual proof with test witnesses. (4) Reclassify the verification tag. The D3 Kani research (R3.3) directly informs this — if Kani proves feasible in CI, some alternatives become unnecessary.

title: R6.2a: Alternative verification for INV-QUERY-001 (Datalog soundness)
description:
INV-QUERY-001 is computationally infeasible for Kani (unbounded Datalog query space). Need alternative verification strategy.

OPTIONS:
A) Downgrade to V:PROP with comprehensive proptest suite:
   - Grammar-based query generation (arb_query())
   - Property: every query result is a valid binding of the where-clauses
   - 10,000+ test iterations with diverse store sizes

B) Manual proof + V:PROOF tag:
   - Prove soundness of semi-naive evaluation for stratified Datalog
   - Reference existing proofs in database theory literature
   - Cite: Abiteboul, Hull, Vianu 'Foundations of Databases' Ch. 13

C) Bounded model checking with reduced state space:
   - Limit to stores with ≤ 10 datoms, queries with ≤ 3 clauses
   - Kani can handle this reduced space

D) TLA+ model checking:
   - Encode query evaluation in TLA+
   - Model check for bounded inputs

RECOMMENDATION: Option A + B (proptest + manual proof reference). This gives both empirical confidence and theoretical grounding.

DELIVERABLE: Verification strategy added to spec/16-verification.md. Updated V: tag.
close_reason: INV-QUERY-001 CALM: Kani feasible (bounded AST, enum tree). False positive in V1 audit.

title: R6.2b: Alternative verification for INV-QUERY-004 (semi-naive correctness)
description:
INV-QUERY-004 is computationally infeasible for Kani (recursive query exploration). Need alternative.

Similar to R6.2a but specifically about semi-naive evaluation correctness:
- Semi-naive produces same results as naive bottom-up evaluation
- Stratification is correct (no stratum references a higher stratum)
- Fixpoint is reached in finite steps

APPROACH: Proptest comparing semi-naive results against naive (brute-force) evaluation for the same query+store. If they always agree, semi-naive is correct.

DELIVERABLE: Verification strategy for INV-QUERY-004.
close_reason: INV-QUERY-004 Branch Visibility: Kani feasible (bounded set operation). V1 audit misidentified as semi-naive.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text: Bilateral traceability means every arrow in the chain SEED.md → spec/ → guide/ → ADRS.md has a reverse arrow. Current gaps: (1) 3 spec ADRs without ADRS.md entries (R6.3a). (2) Some spec elements missing backward SEED.md traces (R6.3b). (3) ~8% of ADRS.md entries are informal (no spec ADR correspondence) (R6.3c). The target is 100% bidirectional coverage. This is not just bookkeeping — it's the traceability constraint (C5) that makes the spec self-verifying. An orphan in either direction means either the spec is over-specified (elements without motivation) or under-specified (motivations without elements).

title: R6.3a: Backport 3 orphan spec ADRs to ADRS.md
description:
Agent 14 identified 3 ADRs that exist in spec/ but have no corresponding entry in ADRS.md. These must be backported to maintain bilateral traceability.

ACTION:
1. Identify the 3 orphan ADRs (from Agent 14's findings)
2. For each: read the spec ADR, extract the decision, rationale, and alternatives
3. Add entry to ADRS.md in the appropriate category (FD/PD/SQ/LD)
4. Ensure the ADRS.md entry cross-references the spec location
5. Ensure the spec ADR cross-references ADRS.md

RATIONALE: ADRS.md is the design decision index. Spec ADRs without ADRS.md entries are invisible to agents that read ADRS.md as their primary decision reference. This violates C5 (traceability).

QUALITY CRITERION: grep -c 'ADR-' ADRS.md matches total spec ADR count.
close_reason: 8 orphan spec ADRs backported to ADRS.md (FD-014, SR-012/013, SQ-011, CR-008/009, CO-015, GU-009)

ALSO: For every trace in SEED→spec direction, verify the reverse exists (spec→SEED). This ensures bilateral convergence.

QUALITY CRITERION: 100% of spec elements have explicit traceability annotation. Zero orphan elements in either direction.

title: R6.3c: Formalize remaining ~8% of ADRS.md entries as spec ADRs
description:
Agent 14 found ~92% ADRS.md formalization rate. ~8% of ADRS.md entries lack corresponding formal ADR elements in spec/.

ACTION:
1. Read ADRS.md — extract all decision IDs (FD-NNN, PD-NNN, SQ-NNN, LD-NNN)
2. For each: check if a corresponding ADR-NAMESPACE-NNN exists in spec/
3. For those without spec counterparts: determine if they SHOULD be formalized
   - If yes: create formal ADR in appropriate spec namespace file
   - If no: document why (may be superseded, may be meta-level not spec-level)

RATIONALE: ADRS.md entries represent decisions that were made during design sessions. Some may be too meta-level for the spec (e.g., 'we chose Rust' is a project decision, not a spec invariant). But any decision that affects the system's behavior or architecture MUST be in spec/.

QUALITY CRITERION: Every behavioral/architectural decision in ADRS.md has a corresponding spec ADR, or an explicit documented reason why not.

title: R6.5: Incorporate audit failure modes into FAILURE_MODES.md
description:
Identify failure modes discovered during the V1 audit and incorporate them into FAILURE_MODES.md.

AUDIT-DISCOVERED FAILURE MODES (to catalog with FM-NNN IDs):
1. Type divergence between documents → implementing agent makes contradictory choices
2. Invariant ID collision (INV-MERGE-008) → traceability graph corruption
3. Spec internal contradiction → implementing agent encounters logical impossibility
4. CRDT cascade breaks commutativity → data corruption in multi-agent merge
5. K_agent epistemological gap → harvest misses uncommitted knowledge
6. Token counting undefined → budget INVs untestable
7. Phantom types → implementing agent encounters undefined types at compile time
8. LWW tie-breaking ambiguity → non-deterministic resolution results
9. MCP protocol underspecification → MCP server doesn't interoperate with clients
10. Verification tag mismatch → wrong verification strategy applied

For each:
1. Assign FM-NNN ID following FAILURE_MODES.md conventions
2. Map to DDIS/Braid mechanism that prevents or detects it
3. Define acceptance criterion with SLA target
4. Cross-reference to the audit finding that discovered it

DELIVERABLE: FAILURE_MODES.md updated with new entries. Cross-references added.

SOURCES: All 14 audit agents.
TRACES: FAILURE_MODES.md, V1_AUDIT_3-3-2026.md.

title: EPIC: Phase 2 — Architecture Decision Resolution
description:
## Purpose

Resolve all architecture-level contradictions between spec and guide that require DESIGN DECISIONS. These are not transcription errors — they are genuine disagreements about system architecture that arose because the spec and guide were authored with different assumptions.

## Why Phase 2 Depends on Phase 1

The implementing agent must have a consistent spec (Phase 1) before architecture decisions can be made against it. A decision made against incorrect Stage assignments may be wrong.

## Key Principle: Spec Leads, Guide Follows

Per CLAUDE.md and DDIS methodology, the spec IS the source of truth. When spec and guide disagree on a contract, the spec wins UNLESS the guide reveals a design insight that should be back-propagated to the spec. The decision process is:
1. Read the spec's Level 2 contract
2. Read the guide's Level 2 contract
3. Determine which is more correct/complete
4. If spec wins: update guide to match
5. If guide reveals improvement: update spec first, then verify guide matches
6. Document the decision as an ADR comment on the bead

## Scope

6 architecture decisions requiring resolution:
- MCP architecture: subprocess vs library call (CRIT-2)
- EntityId field visibility: pub vs private (HIGH-13)
- Schema ownership model (HIGH-11)
- Five-part seed template structure (HIGH-3)
- Quality gate numbering/content (HIGH-4)
- Build order: HARVEST-first vs MERGE-first (HIGH-7)

## Success Criteria

After all children are closed:
- Every spec ↔ guide Level 2 type disagreement is resolved
- Each resolution is documented as an ADR or comment
- No architecture decision left for the implementing agent to discover ad-hoc

## Audit Provenance

Findings from: 14-subagent Fagan inspection audit, 2026-03-03
close_reason: All 6 decisions resolved, all 6 implementation beads closed. ADRs created: INTERFACE-004 (library MCP), STORE-014 (private EntityId), SCHEMA-005 (owned Schema), SEED-004 (unified template). 7-gate pipeline in spec. HARVEST-first build order.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
HOW PHASE 2 SERVES THE ZERO-DEFECT GOAL

Architecture decisions are BIFURCATION POINTS — each one splits the implementation space into two incompatible branches. An implementing agent that encounters an undecided bifurcation must GUESS. Guesses have 50% probability of being wrong. Wrong guesses require REWORK — the antithesis of accretive development.

The cleanroom methodology requires all design decisions to be SETTLED before implementation begins. This is not waterfall thinking — it is recognition that certain decisions have such high blast radius (MCP architecture affects every tool handler, Schema ownership affects every store operation) that deferring them creates more work than deciding them.

Each decision in Phase 2 follows the same protocol:
1. STATE the contradiction (spec says X, guide says Y)
2. ANALYZE the tradeoffs (implications of each choice)
3. RECOMMEND (with rationale grounded in project constraints C1-C7)
4. DECIDE (user confirms or overrides)
5. IMPLEMENT (update the losing document)
6. DOCUMENT (add ADR with alternatives rejected and rationale)

This protocol ensures decisions are TRACEABLE (C5) and FALSIFIABLE (C6) — future agents can understand WHY each choice was made and WHAT WOULD CHANGE if the rationale no longer holds.

title: DECISION: MCP architecture — subprocess vs library call
description:
## Finding ID: CRIT-2

## The Contradiction

### Spec says (spec/14-interface.md §14.4):
"The MCP wrapper invokes a CLI command as a subprocess" — each MCP tool call shells out to the braid binary (e.g., braid transact, braid query). MCP server is a SEPARATE process that spawns CLI subprocesses.

### Guide says (guide/09-interface.md §9.2):
Direct Rust function calls — self.handle_transact(request.params).await — calling kernel functions in-process. MCP server is a SINGLE process that calls kernel directly.

## Why This Matters

These are fundamentally different architectures:

| Aspect | Subprocess model | Library model |
|--------|-----------------|---------------|
| Process isolation | Full — MCP server and kernel in separate processes | None — shared address space |
| Serialization | CLI args → stdout → parse | Direct Rust types |
| Error handling | Exit codes + stderr | Result<T, E> types |
| Performance | Process spawn overhead per call | Near-zero overhead |
| State sharing | None — each call loads/saves store from disk | Shared &Store reference |
| Testing | Integration tests (spawn process) | Unit tests (call function) |
| Deployment | Two binaries or one binary with two modes | Single binary |

## Analysis

### Arguments for Subprocess model (spec):
1. Process isolation prevents MCP protocol bugs from corrupting kernel state
2. Each call is stateless — no session-level bugs
3. Simpler error handling — crash recovery is "process died, restart"
4. Matches how Claude Code MCP servers typically work
5. Easier to test MCP layer independently

### Arguments for Library model (guide):
1. Much faster — no process spawn, no serialization
2. Type-safe — errors are Rust enums, not exit codes
3. Simpler deployment — one binary
4. Can share Store state across calls (important for session coherence)
5. Can use Rust async properly (subprocess model blocks on child process)

### Recommendation: Library model with subprocess testing

Use the library model for production (guide is right), but update the spec:
- MCP server calls kernel functions directly via Rust function calls
- Each tool handler takes a &Store reference, calls kernel, returns ToolResponse
- Integration tests can still test the subprocess path via `braid --mcp-stdio` mode
- The spec should describe the architectural boundary as a trait, not a process boundary

## Action Required

1. User decides: subprocess or library call
2. Whichever wins, update the OTHER document to match
3. If library model: update spec/14-interface.md §14.4 to describe direct function calls
4. If subprocess model: update guide/09-interface.md §9.2 to show subprocess spawning
5. Document the decision as ADR-INTERFACE-004 (or similar)

## Related

- INV-INTERFACE-002 (MCP wrapper)
- INV-INTERFACE-003 (exactly six tools)
- guide/09-interface.md §9.2 (MCPServer struct)
close_reason: DECIDED: Library model — MCP calls kernel functions directly. C1+C4 make Store immutable, process isolation unnecessary. Created ADR-INTERFACE-004, updated INV-INTERFACE-002 and NEG-INTERFACE-002.

comments.author: Willem van Daalenn
comments.text:
ARCHITECTURAL IMPLICATIONS OF EACH CHOICE

SUBPROCESS MODEL: The MCP server becomes a thin JSON-RPC dispatcher that forks braid CLI processes. Architecture: MCP process -> fork(braid transact) -> braid loads store from redb -> executes -> writes result to stdout -> MCP process reads stdout -> returns JSON-RPC response.

Implications: (1) Every tool call has ~50-100ms process spawn overhead (2) Every call re-reads the entire store from disk (no in-memory caching) (3) Error handling via exit codes + stderr parsing is fragile (4) But: process isolation means MCP bugs cannot corrupt the store (5) And: each call is stateless, simplifying reasoning about correctness

LIBRARY MODEL: The MCP server holds a Store reference in memory and dispatches to kernel functions directly. Architecture: MCP server holds &Store -> receives JSON-RPC -> calls kernel::query(&store, expr) -> formats response -> returns JSON-RPC.

Implications: (1) Near-zero overhead per call (no process spawn, no disk I/O for reads) (2) Store loaded once, shared across all calls in a session (3) Error handling via Rust Result types (type-safe, exhaustive) (4) But: bugs in MCP layer can corrupt shared Store (mitigated by &Store immutability from C1) (5) And: session state management becomes the MCP server's responsibility

RECOMMENDATION RATIONALE: The library model is strictly better for Braid because C1 (append-only) + C4 (CRDT merge) mean the Store is effectively immutable — &Store is always valid. Process isolation is solving a problem that the CRDT architecture already solves. The subprocess overhead would make MCP tool calls noticeably slower, degrading agent experience.

title: DECISION: EntityId inner field visibility — pub vs private
description:
## Finding ID: HIGH-13

## The Contradiction

### guide/00-architecture.md:
EntityId(Hash) — PRIVATE inner field. Rationale: content-addressable identity means EntityIds can only be constructed by hashing content. No public constructor from arbitrary bytes. This prevents accidental construction of EntityIds that do not correspond to any content.

### spec/01-store.md:
pub struct EntityId(pub Hash) — PUBLIC inner field. Allows direct access to the underlying hash bytes.

## Analysis

### Private (guide) rationale:
- Enforces INV-STORE-002 at the type level: EntityId construction ONLY via content hashing
- Prevents bugs where someone constructs an EntityId from arbitrary bytes
- The typestate pattern listed in §16.3 says "no public constructor from arbitrary bytes"
- Aligns with C2 (identity by content) — the type system PREVENTS non-content-addressed IDs

### Public (spec) rationale:
- Simpler — no need for accessor methods
- Serialization/deserialization may need direct access
- Testing may need to construct EntityIds from known hashes
- Pattern matching on the inner value

### Recommendation: PRIVATE with accessor

The guide is right. Private inner field with:
- pub fn new(content: &[u8]) -> EntityId — the ONLY constructor, hashes content with BLAKE3
- pub fn as_bytes(&self) -> &[u8; 32] — read-only access to hash bytes (for serialization)
- impl From<[u8; 32]> for EntityId — ONLY for deserialization from storage, marked with a comment

This enforces INV-STORE-002 at compile time (zero runtime cost) and matches §16.3.

## Action Required

1. User confirms: private (guide) or public (spec)?
2. Update the losing document
3. If private wins: update spec/01-store.md Level 2 contract to use private field
4. If public wins: update guide/00-architecture.md and remove typestate claim from spec/16-verification.md §16.3

## Related

- INV-STORE-002 (EntityId construction)
- spec/16-verification.md §16.3 (Typestate Encoding Catalog)
- C2 (Identity by content)
close_reason: DECIDED: Private inner field. EntityId(pub [u8;32]) → EntityId([u8;32]) with from_content()/as_bytes(). Created ADR-STORE-014. Enforces INV-STORE-002 + C2 at compile time.

title: DECISION: Schema ownership model — borrow, copy, or owned
description:
## Finding ID: HIGH-11

## The Three-Way Contradiction

### Spec (spec/03-schema.md):
Schema BORROWS from Store: schema(&self) -> Schema<'"'"'_>
Lifetime-tied to the Store reference. Cannot outlive the borrow.

### Guide (guide/02-schema.md):
Schema is a SEPARATE COPY with its own validation logic. Independent of Store lifetime.

### Architecture (guide/00-architecture.md):
Schema is OWNED BY Store internally. Store.schema field.

## Analysis

In Rust, these are three different memory models:

### Borrow model (spec):
```rust
impl Store {
    pub fn schema(&self) -> Schema<'"'"'_> { /* borrows self */ }
}
```
- Pro: Zero allocation, enforces that schema cannot diverge from store
- Con: Schema cannot be passed around independently, limits API ergonomics
- Con: Cannot hold Schema across a &mut Store borrow (Rust borrow rules)

### Copy model (guide):
```rust
impl Store {
    pub fn schema(&self) -> Schema { /* clones/copies schema data */ }
}
```
- Pro: Independent lifetime, can hold while mutating store
- Con: Allocation per call, can diverge from store after copy
- Con: Violates C3 if schema is treated as separate from store data

### Owned model (architecture):
```rust
pub struct Store {
    schema: Schema,  // owned field
    datoms: BTreeSet<Datom>,
}
```
- Pro: Schema always consistent with store (single owner)
- Con: Need &self to access schema, same borrow issues as borrow model
- Note: This is an INTERNAL implementation detail, not an API contract

### Recommendation: Owned internally, borrow in API

The architecture and spec are actually COMPATIBLE:
- Store OWNS the Schema internally (architecture)
- Store EXPOSES it via borrow: schema(&self) -> &Schema (spec, adjusted to &Schema instead of Schema<'"'"'_>)
- The guide's copy model is wrong — it violates C3 (schema-as-data means schema lives in the store)

This gives us:
- Zero allocation (no copy)
- Schema always consistent with store (owned by store)
- Clean API (&Schema is simple, no lifetime parameter needed since it borrows Store)

## Action Required

1. User confirms: owned+borrow (recommended) vs copy
2. Update guide/02-schema.md to match spec/architecture
3. Clarify in spec/03-schema.md whether Schema<'"'"'_> means &Schema or a custom lifetime-tied type
4. Ensure guide/00-architecture.md type catalog shows Schema as an internal Store field

## Related

- C3 (Schema-as-data)
- INV-SCHEMA-003 (Attribute newtype)
- INV-SCHEMA-004 (Schema monotonicity)
close_reason: DECIDED: Owned internally, borrow in API. Schema<'a> → Schema (owned). Store::schema() returns &Schema. Created ADR-SCHEMA-005. Updated INV-SCHEMA-001 Level 2 contract.
